data.title: Morrisons to sell dozens of pharmacies in scramble to cut costs
data.description:
Morrisons has put dozens of pharmacies up for sale in a fresh effort to cut costs.

The private equity-owned supermarket has launched a sale process after concluding that many of its in-store pharmacies are not financially viable.

As a result of the review, property agents have been tasked with selling some of the pharmacies on a store-by-store basis, rather than the entire portfolio.

Those affected are likely to remain open in stores, but under the brand of any eventual buyer.

The move to sell dozens of pharmacies comes after Morrisons’ losses plunged to £381m last year, fuelled by steep borrowing costs.

Rising debt pressures have led bosses to reduce borrowings, which fell to £3.1bn from £3.5bn last summer.

The sale of pharmacies is the latest attempt to strip back the business and focus increasingly on traditional retail.

A separate restructuring last year triggered the closure of four pharmacies, alongside 52 cafés, 17 convenience stores, 35 meat counters and 13 florists.

This led to more than 300 jobs being put at risk of redundancy, as bosses identified areas “where the costs of operations are significantly out of line with usage, volumes, or the value that customers place on them”.

Rami Baitieh, chief executive of Morrisons, said the cuts were a “necessary part of our plans to renew and reinvigorate Morrisons”.

Morrisons is pursuing a turnaround strategy after Mr Baitieh said the business had been going through a “challenging period”.

The retail boss blamed weaker consumer spending among poorer shoppers, many of whom have been affected by price rises.

This has led to Morrisons struggling to boost its share of the grocery market, which is at 8.4pc.

That is up narrowly from 8.5pc this time last year, although it is well below the 9.9pc it achieved in October 2021, when it was bought by private equity firm Clayton Dubilier & Rice in a £10bn deal.

This has prompted speculation that Morrisons could lose its position as Britain’s fifth-largest supermarket to Lidl, which now boasts 7.7pc of the grocery market.

Morrisons is not alone in seeking to sell off pharmacies, as the sector has been battered by rising costs in recent years.

Around 1,400 pharmacies have shut in England since 2017, according to the National Pharmacy Association, leaving many households unable to access medicine in their areas.

This has been blamed on rising wage costs and a shortfall in how much the NHS pays pharmacies.

Boots has also closed hundreds of pharmacies in recent years, while LloydsPharmacy, which had 138 sites, collapsed in 2023.

Rachel Reeves has piled more pressure on pharmacies over the past year by increasing business rates and the minimum wage.

Supermarkets have also come under pressure from rising taxes under Labour, most notably by the Chancellor’s decision to increase employer National Insurance rates.

Speaking last month, Mr Baitieh pleaded with the Government to avoid hitting retailers with any extra costs.

He said: “I would say, please, no more. This is very important for the market. We expect inflation to go up.”

Morrisons declined to comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Labour has no plan for the worst energy shock of our lives
data.description:
Keep calm and carry on – or something to that effect.

It’s week five of Donald Trump’s Iran excursion, and just when you think the president couldn’t sound any more deranged, he’s now talking about taking Kharg Island by force. 

This is an idea military strategists have compared with anything from a “suicide mission” to a “self-imposed hostage crisis”.

Either that, or Trump could decide to blow the offshore terminal to smithereens instead; such is the clear and rational thinking that pervades the White House these days.

The price of crude oil responded with another 3pc jump on Monday, taking it to $116 (£87.94) at one point – a roughly 50pc increase since hostilities began. Expect it to go much higher if the US puts boots on the ground in Iran.

Meanwhile, there is talk that repairs to damaged energy infrastructure could take between two and five years, prolonging the disruption to global supplies and keeping prices ruinously high.

Indeed, many experts already believe the Middle East conflict is shaping up to be the worst energy shock the world has ever faced.

As Fatih Birol, the head of the International Energy Agency, has warned, the Iran war has knocked out roughly 11 million barrels of oil a day compared with the loss of five million barrels during each of the two crises in 1973 and 1979.

Gas shortages have been similarly devastating, with about 140 billion cubic metres a day lost – double what was removed from the market after Russia’s invasion of Ukraine.

So what, might you ask, is our Government’s plan to address what could prove to be an even bigger blow to our livelihoods than both the pandemic and Vladimir Putin’s imperialist expansion on Europe’s doorstep?

The answer, it seems, is that there isn’t one. On the contrary, ministers are still – publicly, at least – persisting with the absurd stance that it is pretty much “situation normal”.

Yes, contingency plans are being drawn up. “You would expect any government to plan for eventualities where events happen in the world,” Bridget Phillipson, the Education Secretary, said breezily at the weekend.

However, “people should be reassured, ahead of the Easter holidays, just to carry on as things are”, she added, seemingly oblivious to the irony of how anxiety-inducing such words are.

While ministers attempt their desperate “nothing to see here” act for fear of “scaring people”, as Phillipson patronisingly puts it, those same people can turn on the news and see that two things are happening.

The first is the supply squeeze becoming more acute worldwide. Bangladesh may have only a few weeks of oil left, swathes of Southeast Asia have only a few months’ worth of supplies at best, parts of Africa are experiencing shortages, and so too are Australia and New Zealand.

True, many of the countries facing a critical supply squeeze are more reliant on Middle Eastern oil, while we, on the other hand, get most of our crude from the US and Norway. 

However, much more of our refined fuels are imported, which is why the price of diesel has gone up 26pc versus 15pc for unleaded petrol, so it’s only a matter of time before Europe is next.

Indeed, industry figures think the crunch is just around the corner. Wael Sawan, Shell’s chief executive, has warned of energy and fuel shortages by mid-April. That’s a fortnight away. 

Traders and fuel suppliers estimate that diesel stockpiles could halve by mid-May.

I know who I’m inclined to listen to, and it’s not the Education Secretary, nor any Cabinet member, for that matter. The Government is being far too complacent.

The second thing that people in this country can see is that while ministers dilly-dally, a growing number of countries are acting quickly and decisively, starting with sweeping tax cuts to soften the hit to household incomes.

Spain’s socialist government was among the first to move, cutting VAT on fuel, electricity and gas by more than half to 10pc as part of a €5bn (£3.8bn) emergency support package. 

Ireland cut fuel duty by a fifth, while the Polish government slashed VAT on fuels from 23pc to 8pc, and reduced excise taxes to the lowest level permitted by Brussels.

At the weekend, Anthony Albanese, the Australian prime minister, joined in by halving fuel duty – a move expected to save motorists 26 cents a litre when they fill up their tanks – as well as unveiling a new four-stage fuel security plan to manage supply.

Other than support for those reliant on heating oil, there has been no such relief here.

Steps to bring down both energy and fuel consumption are also being rapidly introduced, though again not in the UK. 

India has shut down factories, Australia has introduced free public transport to discourage people from driving, civil servants in the Philippines are working a four-day week, and Slovenia has become the first European country to introduce rationing.

None of these represents a silver bullet, but surely any action is better than none at all? Why not start here by designating filling stations for emergency vehicles and prioritising diesel for commercial vehicles?

It is ludicrous for our Government to pretend we are somehow immune to the pressures being felt everywhere else, and it is even sillier to think we can’t handle a few small sacrifices for the greater good of the country. 

Reducing unnecessary journeys, turning off the lights, turning down the heating and going on fewer holidays would be nothing compared to the restrictions everyone endured during Covid.

Our thoroughly forgettable Prime Minister would much rather have more pointless meetings than take tough decisions – the latest with representatives from the shipping, insurance and energy industries to discuss the “importance of working closely with the private sector in tackling the crisis” – whatever that means.

For Rachel Reeves, it has reached the humiliating point where bosses are avoiding her altogether. 

First, the petrol retailers gave the Chancellor the cold shoulder because of accusations of “profiteering”, then several supermarket bosses were too busy to discuss rising food prices with her. Devoid of ideas, this Government has been reduced to populist show trials.

At the very least, it can scrap the planned fuel duty rise, yet Phillipson insists there is “no need” to. It is a farcical position to take, and it sets the scene for yet another screeching Labour policy reversal in the coming weeks. 

No wonder business bosses have stopped listening. Then again, hasn’t everyone?
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: The IMF is wrong. Britain is safe from the worst of the Gulf energy shock
data.description:
One myth never dies: markets, think tanks and talking heads take it for granted that the UK is more vulnerable to fossil-fuel shocks than other advanced economies and will suffer greater damage from the unfolding disaster in the Gulf.

The International Monetary Fund has chipped in, warning that the UK is “especially exposed” because of its heavy reliance on gas-fired power. Really?

The share of gas in the UK’s electricity generation over the year to April has collapsed to 27.3pc. It will continue to drop like a stone this year as the dash for wind and solar adds almost a gigawatt of capacity each month between now and October. The gas share was 41.8pc in early 2022 when Russia invaded Ukraine.

The same outdated thinking seems to underpin the latest downgrade by the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD), which cut its forecast for the UK more than any other G20 country, including Asian states that have had to introduce emergency measures. Large parts of Korea’s petrochemical industry are operating at sharply reduced run rates, and helium is hard to come by for Korea’s semiconductor industry.

The UK has cut gas use by two-fifths since 2004 – yes, partly because of industrial shrinkage – and is today at roughly the same level of dependency as Germany, Italy, Japan or Korea. Unlike these countries, it produces 35pc to 40pc of its own net oil and gas supply. This acts as a sort of macroeconomic buffer via tax revenue and slower leakage in the balance of trade.

We still have a defective pricing mechanism that lets the last marginal watt of gas-fired power, from the least efficient old clunker, set the electricity price for the whole country. But the toxic effects are diminishing fast. Gas set the marginal price 97pc of the time in 2021.

Last month, it was down to two-thirds. The figure is in free fall as cheaper batteries displace gas for short-term backup.

The Gulf energy shock is not a gas story in any case. It is chiefly a supply crisis for oil, plastics, fertiliser, helium, sulphur and aluminium. The British economy is not heavily geared to these products. The US itself is most certainly, and in ways Donald Trump’s inner circle never imagined.

The US closed down its Federal Helium Reserve in 2024, deeming it a costly anachronism. Whoops. Almost two-fifths of the world’s helium supply is now locked up in Qatar, and you cannot make AI chips or run superconducting magnets without this element, the coldest gas on Earth.

It is true that no tanker of liquefied natural gas (LNG) has left the Gulf since Feb 28. Trump has no idea how to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, and Iran’s Revolutionary Guard Corps has no incentive to oblige him.

None of these giant combustible ships is going to risk the journey for a long time. A drone strike is death for the crew.

But let us clarify a prevalent misunderstanding. While Qatar and the Emirates account for one-fifth of the world’s LNG, which equals only 3pc of the world’s gas consumption. Most gas still runs on regional pipelines.

Before the war, the International Energy Agency said the LNG industry was heading into a structural glut through the late 2020s due to overbuilding of export terminals in the US, Russia, Qatar and Mexico. In short, the saturated global market can more or less handle the total loss of LNG from the Gulf.

We will not avoid a mini-shock. The benchmark European gas contract was €25 per megawatt hour in January, and it is around €52 now; Standard Chartered is eyeing €80 this year.

The UK has subcontracted its gas storage to Germany and the Netherlands. Both are down to wafer-thin levels after the cold winter. Inventories will have to be replenished at today’s inflated prices before next winter. But we are not looking at anything close to the €300-plus levels of mid-2022.

The much larger crisis is in the oil market. A fifth of the world’s supply comes from the Gulf, and most remains trapped, even after rerouting some supply through (vulnerable) pipelines to the Red Sea and the Gulf of Oman.

“We don’t need their oil,” said Trump. If that is so, why has Texas crude doubled since early February from $55 to $111 and even pushed past Brent crude? Why has US jet fuel jumped from $2.27 to $4.64?

Why is United Airlines having to cancel internal US flights? Why has the average US petrol price jumped from $2.92 to $4.02 a gallon? Why are Prairie farmers paying $5.07 for diesel?

Trump is discovering that a shock in one corner of the global petroleum markets ricochets almost instantly into every other part, unless you are a totalitarian state such as China, with price controls and deep strategic reserves. The US is a net oil exporter, but it still imports eight million barrels a day, mostly heavy crude, to balance its refineries.

American oil companies may be making windfall gains, but American consumers are on the other side of the trade; it is they who will determine Trump’s fate in the midterm elections.

Clearly, the UK has made many mistakes in its energy policy. It still relies too much on gas for buildings and home heating, the predictable consequence of piling levies on electricity while shielding – and bailing out – gas users. 

The price ratio of electricity to gas is four to one, double the levels of electrostate success stories.

It was a bad idea to push our refiners to the wall, firstly by denying them lower industrial electricity costs under the Supercharger scheme. Secondly, by imposing high carbon fees on them while allowing imports of refined products from countries with no carbon tax. 

That outsources our CO2 emissions and flatters our green vanity. Net zero foes are right about that.

It was careless to allow the closure of the Grangemouth and Lindsey refineries last year, reducing the UK’s total to four refineries and increasing the country’s reliance on jet fuel imports to 80pc. The country now has a jet fuel supply scare and a headache of bad headlines.

But what matters for the wider economy is how much energy is needed per unit of GDP, and on this measure, the UK scores well.

World Bank data show – very broad brush – that the UK uses two megajoules per unit of GDP, compared with three for the OECD bloc, four for the US, five for the world, six for China and eight for Russia.

Britons drive half as far as Americans, and their cars are twice as efficient. There are already 1.9 million electric vehicles on the road, and this alone displaces 75,000 barrels of oil a day.

So what explains the frightening rise in 10-year gilt yields and the widening premium over peers? There is a technical reason.

David Owen from Saltmarsh Economics said European funds had been recycling the EU’s trade surplus into UK gilts to the tune of £150bn a year in the hunt for yield. 

“These are huge balances, and whenever there is a risk-off episode, they pull their money back, and gilts get beaten up. It’s the home-bias effect,” he said.

“I think the UK gets a bad press. The debt ratio is 100pc of GDP: the US is heading for 150pc and has a budget deficit of 8pc. So, which has the real problem?”

Of course, bond vigilantes want a fat risk premium against the fiscal fantasies of the British political Left. Investors justifiably fear that Angela Rayner and her fellow plotters in the Labour Party will either compel the Government to let rip with another exorbitant energy bailout or seize power and do it themselves.

They are now competing with Zack Polanski’s Green Party, who have migrated so far from ecology to socialist hedonism that they want giant subsidies for fossil fuels.

There is much to be alarmed about, but it is not clear to me that any of the big open democracies are in better political shape or that France, Italy, the US and Japan have their fiscal houses in order.

British catastrophism is overcooked. As for the idea that Gulf War III will hit Britain harder than any other G20 country, may I suggest that such an implausible analysis needs a brutal interrogation?

data.title: Majority of Labour voters back drilling in the North Sea
data.description:
A majority of Labour voters support drilling for oil in the North Sea, a new poll has found.

In a blow to Ed Miliband, a survey has revealed that supporters of drilling outnumber the opponents by more than two to one amongst his party’s voters.

The Energy Secretary has repeatedly dismissed suggestions that Britain should ramp up domestic oil and gas production, even as the crisis in the Middle East has piled pressure on global supplies.

But a poll by campaign group Looking for Growth suggests Labour’s supporters – and a majority of voters in the country – disagree with him.

The poll found that support for drilling for oil in the North Sea stood at about 57pc amongst all voters, compared to only 15pc who were against.

Within this, support amongst Labour voters stood at 51pc while 21pc were opposed. Another 22pc said they neither agreed nor disagreed, with 5pc saying they didn’t know.

Even amongst backers of the eco-focused Green Party, supporters for North Sea drilling narrowly outnumbered opponents by 38pc to 33pc.

Support amongst Conservative and Reform voters topped 70pc.

Some 49pc of Labour voters also indicated they supported onshore fracking of oil and gas – although just 40pc were happy for it to take place in their area.

Lawrence Newport, the chief executive of Looking for Growth, said: “There has been no government plan to reduce bills, have abundant energy and stable long-term power.

“Instead, politicians have adopted policies that have left the lives of millions across the country at the mercy of international events.

“When successive governments give in to the consultants, lawyers and lobbyists, the energy bills of the British people pay the price.

“This new polling shows that voters of all parties want a broken system fixed and to be prioritised again.”

Looking for Growth’s poll also asked voters about nuclear power and found that 61pc supported it, compared to 14pc who opposed it.

The debate over North Sea drilling has intensified in recent weeks following the US-Iran conflict, which sent energy prices soaring.

The RAC said on Tuesday the crisis had pushed the cost of filling up a typical family car with diesel beyond £100 for the first time since 2022.

The cost of filling up a petrol car has similarly increased by around £11, bringing the total to £85.

Most supporters of increasing domestic oil and gas production say it is unlikely to directly lower costs, as both commodities are priced on international markets, but that it could improve energy security, generate higher tax revenues and protect jobs.

Mr Miliband has introduced a ban on new drilling in the North Sea and has so far resisted pressure to rethink his position.

Speaking to Labour MPs this month, he reportedly said: “Gas is bought and sold on the international market, so whether it comes from the North Sea or is imported, it is charged at the same price.”

He went on to praise the amount of money raised by a renewable energy auction earlier this year, saying technology such as wind turbines and solar panels was “homegrown power that we can rely on”.

The Conservatives accused the Energy Secretary of dogma and called on the Government to cut taxes on domestic energy production.

Claire Coutinho, the shadow energy secretary, said earlier this week: “It is unforgivable that Labour are continuing with Ed Miliband’s mad plan to shut down our own energy supplies in the North Sea in the middle of an energy crisis.

“Keir Starmer is too weak to stand up to Ed Miliband, who has allowed radical activists to make us weaker, poorer and more dependent on foreign imports with higher emissions through endless lawfare.”

A Government spokesman said: “Regardless of where it comes from, oil and gas is sold on international markets, which set the price for British billpayers – making us a price taker.

“The only way to truly protect ourselves from these price spikes is to get off the rollercoaster of fossil fuel markets.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: North Sea gas ‘saves Britain billions a year’
data.description:
North Sea natural gas has been found to have saved Britain billions last year, increasing pressure on the Government to boost production during the Iran war.

Tapping into its own resources of offshore gas instead of paying for imported liquefied natural gas (LNG) shipments saved the UK about £2.5bn last year, according to analysis from investment bank Stifel.

The bank said savings on UK-produced gas would be “substantially higher” this year because prices have surged during the Middle East conflict.

Gas prices have soared by 56pc since the start of the war as the threat of strikes has deterred tankers from passing through the Strait of Hormuz, where a fifth of global exports usually pass.

Meanwhile, missile damage at Qatar’s Ras Laffan plant, which produces a fifth of the world’s LNG, is expected to take three to five years to repair.

Stifel’s analysis will pile further pressure on Ed Miliband, who has banned new exploration in British waters and has so far rejected calls to extract more fossil fuels from the North Sea because of his net zero push.

The Energy Secretary has claimed that awarding new licences for North Sea drilling would do little to lower prices.

However, Chris Wheaton, an analyst at Stifel, said it was astonishing that ministers were not ramping up production of North Sea gas, asking: “Doesn’t the Government realise there’s a war on?”

“Unsurprisingly – and we are frankly astonished that this has to be stated – the UK’s own North Sea gas is cheaper than imported LNG, and we estimate this saved the UK £2.5bn in 2025 alone,” he said. “With the increase in global LNG prices due to the Persian Gulf conflict, we expect that amount will be substantially higher in 2026.”

The North Sea is still the UK’s biggest gas source, accounting for around 45pc of its supplies. The other 35pc comes from piped gas from Norway, while 20pc is LNG imports.

‘Maximise production’

The Climate Change Commission has said Britain will still need oil and gas beyond 2050.

According to Stifel, this imported LNG has been, on average, 18p per therm more expensive than North Sea gas over the past few years, with this gap helping Britain save about £2.5bn in 2025.

“To minimise the cost of energy for the UK, the UK needs to maximise its own gas production,” Mr Wheaton added.

The Iran war has increased pressure on Mr Miliband to grant new licences for the Rosebank oil field and the Jackdaw gas field in the North Sea.

His opposition has prompted a growing backbench rebellion among Labour MPs, who are calling for a rethink of his net zero agenda.

Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, opened up a rift with the Energy Secretary last week, saying she was “very happy” to support drilling, which she said created “jobs and tax revenue”.

Manufacturing bosses and unions have also warned that factories will struggle to survive more cost pressures.

Stifel urged the Energy Secretary to reform the windfall tax on oil and gas producers, which it said was “taxing the UK’s own gas production to destruction”.

Mr Wheaton said this was “a decision which makes no sense to us whatsoever, as it increases the amount of expensive LNG imports”.

Offshore Energies UK has predicted the so-called Energy Profits Levy will see UK oil and gas output plunge by 40pc by the end of the decade.

Energy prices have moved violently during the past month with the onset of the Iran war. Brent crude oil, the international benchmark, rose to $111 a barrel on Monday before paring gains to $108.

Traders are watching for signs of easing tensions between the US and Iran after reports that both sides are working towards a ceasefire.

New analysis by Kpler, an energy research group, published on Monday, suggested oil prices would have to rise to between $160 and $170 to balance supply.

This would help cut demand by around six million barrels a day – global demand is around 104 million barrels a day – and reduce the risk of shortages.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Building in London is becoming impossible, warns housing giant
data.description:
Building homes in London is becoming impossible because of high taxes and red tape, a major housebuilder has warned.

Berkeley Group said on Wednesday that it was halting new land purchases and reducing investment in its building projects, most of which are in the capital or other urban areas.

The company said the conflict in Iran, which is pushing up costs, and a deteriorating economic outlook contributed to its decision to pull back from the market.

However, Rob Perrins, the chief executive of Berkeley, criticised red tape and taxes that he said risked making London effectively uninvestable.

Berkeley said land prices were becoming “overheated” because it was too easy for other businesses to bid for land. Mr Perrins said that Big Yellow Group, the self-storage giant, is his “biggest competitor at the moment”.

“The Government needs to prioritise housebuilding land and not allow Big Yellow to buy all the land, because London will have no [new] homes, fundamentally, if we carry on as we are,” he said.

“You can’t keep increasing taxation on fewer and fewer houses. We have three types of taxes that are too high – development tax, customer-facing tax and corporate tax, which other land uses don’t get.”

In its update to the market, Berkeley criticised “an unprecedented increase in cost and regulation, at a time of increasing interest rates and faltering consumer confidence, amidst prolonged geopolitical and macro-economic volatility and uncertainty”.

Mr Perrins criticised the building safety levy, a tax aimed at funding cladding remediation work and the community infrastructure levy, a charge that councils use to fund infrastructure such as schools and health services.

He said: “The building safety levy should be dropped. We already pay a residential property developer tax of 4pc. Our corporation tax is [effectively] 29pc. We’ve got all the employee taxes.

“There’s a huge amount of what I call customer-facing taxes [such as] stamp duty, and then there’s a huge amount of development tax.

“The Government is not focusing and prioritising affordable housing, [but] focusing on tax-raising measures like the Community Infrastructure Levy.”

Building safety planning rules had added around a year to the time taken to gain planning approval and begin construction on projects, Berkeley said.

Mr Perrins said the quagmire in London meant Labour’s manifesto pledge to build 1.5 million homes by the end of this parliament was looking increasingly far-fetched.

Underpinning that ambition is the Mayor of London’s target to build 88,000 homes a year in the capital.

Mr Perrins said: “If London has a requirement for 88,000 homes, and we’re building 10pc of those, it probably sums up how difficult it is for the Government to hit its target. It just puts it in complete context.”

Steve Reed, the Housing Secretary and Sir Sadiq Khan, the Mayor of London, unveiled an emergency package to boost housebuilding in the capital in October. Reforms include cutting the proportion of affordable homes required for a site from 35pc to 20pc.

Mr Perrins said the temporary measures were to be “applauded”, but warned their success would depend entirely on how “forward-thinking” each borough is.

“My worry is, if boroughs don’t want to follow the messaging, it will not change behaviours on the ground,” he said.

“From a policy point of view, I can’t fault [the measures] … but if local authorities won’t implement them, then it will not translate into new houses quickly.”

He pointed to recent efforts to build 586 homes in Kingston, which were refused by the council after five years of negotiations.

Mr Perrins said the Government needed to consider more measures, such as reducing taxes and regulation on builders, and bringing in demand stimulus through Help to Buy-style schemes for buyers and stamp duty reductions.

Shares in Berkeley plummeted by nearly 18pc on Wednesday morning, marking the steepest fall in a decade. The stock has fallen 24pc over the last month.

The housebuilder cut its profit forecasts following the update. It now expects to generate £1.4bn of pre-tax profit in the four years between 2027 and 2030, roughly equivalent to £350m a year.

Anthony Codling of RBC Capital Markets said the new forecast was around a third lower than previous estimates.

Berkeley said it would redirect its focus to developing its existing land holdings and buying plots through joint-venture agreements, rather than on its own.

Its existing land holdings consist of sites for more than 50,000 homes, with a further pipeline of more than 10,000 in London and the South East.

Aynsley Lammin of Investec said London was the “most difficult area” in the UK for housebuilding and said Berkeley’s decisions were “reflective of the difficult situations” builders face in the capital.

“The affordability constraints are greater. If you’re a first-time buyer, just the hurdle to raise a deposit to get the mortgage is that much more difficult in London,” he said.

Data published by Nationwide in January showed the average first-time buyer must put down a deposit of £44,800 for a home, well above the national average of £23,000.

Mr Lammin said: “Investor appetite has waned as well, particularly from overseas investors, with all of the regulations on investment properties, buy-to-let - that’s been a big factor for Berkeley Group. London was more of an investor market and that’s become very difficult.

“Developers also face more regulatory and cost burdens in London… Some of it is obviously economy-wide, but it’s concentrated and magnified in London. Everything just becomes that much more difficult.”

data.title: Tudor manor refurb plan branded ‘poor taxidermy’
data.description:
Plans to redevelop a Tudor manor house have been criticised as “poor taxidermy” because the interior is expected to be completely rebuilt.

The derelict Winstanley Hall, near Wigan, which dates back to around 1573, has been empty since previous regeneration plans fell through 40 years ago.

However, new plans to renovate the site and turn the building into 36 apartments and houses have been criticised by neighbours.

Syd Cottle, from the Save Winstanley Greenbelt campaign, said: “Only the facade will be retained, so what you’ll have is a brand new building replacing the internal heritage aspects of the structure.

“I compare it to the building equivalent of poor taxidermy.”

Winstanley Hall was owned for 400 years by the Bankes, a successful coal-mining family during the 18th and early 19th centuries. The house was last occupied in the mid-1980s.

Since then, the structure has deteriorated significantly from a lack of maintenance and slates being stolen from the roof, which led to water damage and the collapse of the building’s interior.

A spokesman from Historic England said: “We are pleased to finally see a proposal for what remains of the house and outbuildings to be retained and converted to new homes by a developer.

“However, we remain concerned that the site has been allowed to deteriorate for so many years to its current condition – despite its high national listed status.”

According to Historic England, Grade II* buildings are particularly important buildings of more than special interest. Around 5.8 per cent of listed buildings are Grade II*.

Last year, Kingswood Homes bought the site and has since submitted plans to Wigan council to redevelop it and create nearly 400 new homes.

Locals have objected to the plans, and a petition regarding the proposal has received more than 1,000 signatures.

Deborah Orr, from the Save Winstanley Greenbelt campaign, said there seemed to be a “build, baby, build ethos”, which “feels like it is not properly thought out”.

She said: “We don’t want them to build loads of houses that are not going to be sustainable, will cause flooding, traffic, overcrowding and put pressure on our services.

“The area already floods a lot. Where is the analysis of adding a concrete jungle on that?”

Hayley Pierce, the Green Party candidate for Standish ward in Wigan, said the plans would see the old hall “gutted”.

She added: “The planned proposal for development on the site on and around Winstanley Hall is not a restoration that would bring the hall into use for the community, it is a plan to gut the old hall and fill it with new build flats.”

‘Last chance of being saved’

Paul Jones, the managing director of Kingswood Homes, said: “We are trying to save the hall and bring forward social housing as we are in a time where there’s a massive housing shortage.

“We want to restore the hall and save it, and it is its last chance of being saved.

“People can claim that it’s not a restoration, but Historic England has said it is a restoration and it is supportive of it.

“All those concerns will be assessed by the council as part of the application. If there is an impact, then it has to be mitigated.”

Aidan Thatcher, director of place at Wigan council, said: “At this stage, no decisions have been made regarding the developer’s plans at Winstanley Hall and the surrounding area. 

“We are committed to a thorough and transparent planning process and will review all relevant information, including views from the public, before making a final decision.”

data.title: Sadiq Khan could wage war on ‘Chelsea tractor’ owners with new charges
data.description:
Sir Sadiq Khan is considering waging a war on “Chelsea tractor” owners with new charges, a new document suggests.

Transport for London’s (TfL) Vision Zero action plan claimed there was a “pressing need for targeted safety interventions” against larger vehicles.

It stated the size of large SUVs – nicknamed “Chelsea tractors” – made roads “more hazardous for everybody outside the vehicle” because they “significantly increase the severity of collisions”.

The plan is part of Sir Sadiq’s efforts to eliminate deaths and serious injuries on London’s roads by 2041.

There is no specific definition of an SUV, but it is a term given to vehicles that are generally taller, wider, heavier and less fuel-efficient than traditional cars. Critics have pointed out that electric vehicles (EVs) are just as heavy, but would not be affected by the plans.

Thomas Turrell, a member of the London Assembly, said: “This family car tax is a double standard. The mayor will not go after his precious heavy EV cars with this regressive, backwards tax, instead choosing to hammer families.

“His anti-car zealotry will not stop here. If he truly believes vehicles should be taxed for being heavier, he should be consistent rather than money-grabbing.”

Mike Hawes, the chief executive of the Society of Motor Manufacturers and Traders, said all cars sold in the UK “regardless of size” must meet “exacting safety and pedestrian protection standards”.

He added: “Singling out specific cars based on size restricts consumer choice and would unfairly penalise the many drivers who require a larger vehicle for essential mobility.”

No proposals about possible charges for SUVs have been put forward yet.

SUVs account for about a third of all new cars sold in the UK. They have grown in popularity in recent years, with many drivers favouring their higher seating position.

The Vision Zero document claimed “oversized” vehicles such as SUVs made it “harder for drivers to see people walking and cycling nearby”, adding that children were “more easily hidden from a driver’s view”.

It stated that in Britain, SUVs were 14 per cent more likely to kill pedestrians and cyclists than other passenger cars if there was a collision, and 77 per cent more likely to kill children.

Punishing London’s motorists

Sir Sadiq expanded the ultra-low-emission zone to cover the whole of London in August 2023, which requires drivers of vehicles that do not meet emissions standards to pay a daily fee of £12.50.

London also has an £18 congestion charge for driving in a zone in the centre of the capital at certain times.

Sir Sadiq has faced sustained criticism from ordinary Londoners that he ignores their concerns about schemes that punish motorists.

One such scheme was formally approved this week, which involves the construction of a cycle path at the Holland Park roundabout in west London, one of the capital’s busiest road interchanges.

Residents have repeatedly objected to the £6m scheme and have claimed that TfL has cited false statistics generated to justify the works.

Ruth Saunders, a member of the Save Our Streets group based near the roundabout, said: “This scheme is designed to benefit a small number of commuter cyclists travelling through the borough for a few hours each day, to the detriment of local residents and other road users.”

Regarding the potential plans to tax SUVs, a spokesman for Sir Sadiq claimed: “There is growing evidence about the safety risks of large SUVs, which are becoming more common on London’s roads.

“The mayor has asked TfL to undertake detailed analysis of the safety risks posed by large SUVs and their wider impact on London’s roads.

“This could then inform any future policy proposals in London or recommendations to Government or the sector.

“As the numbers of large SUVs increase on London’s roads, it is right that TfL consider their impact on safety and how they affect other road users.”

data.title: Net zero zealots are putting Gen Z off building planes
data.description:
Britain’s aerospace companies are gearing up for their biggest boom in a generation.

Record airliner orders will require plane makers and their suppliers to lift production by a fifth in the next few years, creating well-paid jobs for thousands of workers.

Yet a UK aerospace industry that produced the jet engine, the Harrier jump jet and Concorde is being hampered by a growing recruitment crisis.

Years of demonisation by green campaigners such as Greta Thunberg for its inability to wean itself off fossil fuels are beginning to take their toll as young people turn away from careers in industry.

A lack of inspirational new aircraft and the allure of rival jobs in technology and AI are compounding the situation, leaving even the biggest firms struggling to hire.

Airbus, which employs 15,000 people in the UK, is resorting to converting one of its Beluga wing transporters into a recruitment centre in an effort to bolster its appeal to Gen Z.

The firm will fit the plane with classrooms and a mock-up of its assembly line as it seeks to recruit hundreds of apprentices a year to the Broughton wing plant in North Wales.

Bosses are hoping that Nasa’s Artemis programme to return humans to the Moon by 2028 will also help restore some of the wider industry’s lost glamour.

Richard Gardner, the honorary president of Farnborough Air Sciences Trust, said Gen Z had been turned off aviation by eco-zealots who wrongly condemn the sector for its environmental impact.

He suggested the problem starts in school, where children are increasingly taught about the impacts of climate change.

He said: “We talk about the word brainwashing, which is quite emotive for some people, but that is what it is.

“The teaching unions are very influential in terms of the way that profession is going, and they are pretty green in their tendencies.”

Gardner added: “I think they mostly buy into these green policies, science and economics teachers aside, and that aerospace and defence are therefore not high in their career recommendations.”

He said the recent rise of the Green Party as an electoral force is also a “big worry” for aviation.

Green policies include a commitment to banning domestic flights for journeys that could be made by train, halting airport expansion and imposing a tax on frequent travellers.

Kevin Craven, the chief executive of ADS, the trade group for aerospace, defence and space firms, said that while the industry might not be decarbonising fast enough for some young people, its focus on reducing emissions may be failing to motivate others.

He said: “There is a feeling that aerospace has faded into the background a bit because there are no longer any mountains being climbed.

“Concorde, the Comet, the jet engine and the Harrier were all British firsts that really excited and inspired people.”

Craven added: “All new aircraft are really a cylinder with wings and look approximately the same. There are incremental advances which are huge for sustainability, but it’s hard to see that progression.

“There is also huge competition from other high-tech arenas – particularly AI – and, more generally, the City, where vast swathes of money are available to the best and brightest.”

Mr Craven said it was galling to see tech companies recruiting the most able engineers, even though their electricity consumption is set to double by 2030, creating clear parallels with the net zero challenges facing aviation.

He said: “When you look at cloud computing, on the other side of it is a dirty big data centre requiring huge amounts of power and huge amounts of cooling.”

‘Incredibly competitive world’

Sue Partridge, the UK country manager for Airbus, said the world’s biggest plane maker had turned to initiatives such as the Beluga recruitment project as it prepares to deliver 1,000 jet planes a year as early as 2027, up from 793 in 2025.

Airbus’s Filton, Gloucestershire, wing design and development centre near Bristol faces competition for A-level students and graduate recruits from a broad spectrum of rival employers, she said.

The firm, which has trained 4,000 British recruits in the past decade, also faces a fight to hire 16-year-old apprentices for its assembly line in Broughton, Flintshire, with Merseyside’s carmakers and the cluster of green energy firms on nearby Deeside, Wales.

She said: “It’s an incredibly competitive world out there, and we need to do everything we can to attract talent and secure those skills we are going to need for the future.

“But it is difficult, particularly in newer digital skill areas, where we are competing with Google and companies like that, which was never the case in the past.”

Partridge added: “With the younger cohort, the challenge is attracting them to the industry in the first place and inspiring them so that they want to actually build aeroplanes. They get influenced in their career choice at an early age.

“A few years ago, all the stories were about flight shaming, and aerospace was the bad guy. That’s why we want to get into schools so they really connect with Airbus and aerospace manufacturing.”

The Beluga, named after the white whale whose bloated fuselage it resembles, garners rare attention for a modern plane and would be a formidable tool in encouraging take-up of Stem (science, technology, engineering and mathematics) subjects, Ms Partridge said.

“There are still inspirational stories we can tell, and I think the Beluga is one,” she said. “We know people love the plane, and we can use that as a symbol.

“It’s an iconic aircraft, and if you see one in the sky, for me, it’s just as impactful as Concorde used to be. We already have a Concorde near Filton, South Gloucestershire, and we can use this in the same way.”

Mr Craven said the development of so-called flying taxis, in which Britain is playing a leading role, is one area that should resonate with younger people.

That is largely “because they will be silent, and there won’t be obvious pollutants pouring out of the back”.

However, it is not clear how big the market for the electric vertical take-off craft will be, with some industry observers concerned that they will be limited to whisking the very rich to and from first-class flights, a role hardly likely to endear them to Gen Z.

Industry experts hope that a new era of manned space travel could achieve greater cut-through.

Elon Musk’s SpaceX has made space a “much closer frontier”, while the Artemis missions have the potential to capture hearts and minds just as the Apollo programme did in the 1960s and 1970s, said Mr Craven.

He said: “Aerospace and space are joined at the hip, and whenever we ask an astronaut to an event, there is a frisson of excitement. People want heroes, and that combination of high-tech and courage is the ultimate inspiration.

“I can remember watching the Moon landing on a black and white TV with a 10-inch screen in the middle of Africa. It’s probably why I ended up as an engineer.”

data.title: The investment gems hidden in Britain’s net zero drive
data.description:
Given the events in the Gulf, one might suppose that the UK’s diverse renewable energy sector, centred on alternative investment trusts, would be surging ahead.

Sadly, you’d be mistaken. To be fair, some funds have recorded meagre gains in the last month, and overall, the sector has avoided the significant losses seen in the wider market.

However, the sector overall has averaged a gain of just 1pc so far this year and an average decline of 0.4pc since the war began.

Politically inspired critics of renewable energy will no doubt rush to judge.

But in reality, a rather long list of parochial, very specific issues has undermined the sector, helping push the three-year return to a queasy 28pc loss.

I would argue that there is a distinct possibility that we may have seen the worst, with a handful of funds offering decent income opportunities for investors, but it’s also true that the challenges facing the space might worsen in the short term.

What went wrong? On paper, the income-driven, renewables fund sector – worth about £10bn in gross assets and the envy of much of Europe – should be very boring and reliable.

It should churn out a predictable income from wind and solar assets backed by government schemes such as Renewable Obligation Certificates (ROCs) and Feed-in tariffs (FIT).

It’s true that fixed revenues account for something between 60pc and 80pc of the revenues of the 10 funds that dominate the space, with ROCs a chunky proportion of that income.

But it’s also true that the variable price of energy and the amount of energy produced do have an impact, and both have been trending down – largely.

Until Iran, the various forward power price curves used in valuation models had also been trending down, which has indirectly fed through to a likely lower Ofgem price cap for UK consumers in the next quarter.

But the killer blow was government policy. Last year, the Department for Energy Security and Net Zero launched a consultation on changing the basis of payments (ROCs and FITs), which resulted in a switch from the higher RPI rate of inflation to CPI from April 2026.

This move was not as bad as many in the industry feared – there was an even worse option – but the whole process undermined investor confidence mightily.

And talking of investors, they were also getting increasingly worried about the cost of refinancing the debt used in these structures, an issue that impacts many other sectors including real estate.

The average cost of debt across most renewable funds is between 3.6pc and 4.6pc per annum. A few funds have meaningful amounts of debt and frequently expensive revolving credit facilities, due to be rescheduled in the next few years.

Not unsurprisingly, investors put two and two together and worried that the rising cost of capital might threaten the generous dividends.

That in turn exposed a slightly hidden secret: where’s the growth coming from? On paper, the underlying energy assets seem like cleverly engineered evergreen cash machines, just churning out subsidy-backed income, but the assets have a fixed life, and in most cases, a fixed life of contracts.

What happens when those contracts finish, and the assets need replacing? Where’s the cash going to come from to renew the asset base if you’re paying it all out as dividend income?

Just to make matters worse, worries about the sustainability of dividend income intensified during the last few months just as two-year and 10-year gilt yields rose. These higher yields are a double whammy.

First, they increase the risk-free rates built into the valuation models, and secondly, they make the dividends paid out by many funds less attractive, especially if you can get, say, 5pc for lending to the Government long term and risk-free (although fans of the sector might argue that gilt yields are fixed whereas many fund dividends might grow with inflation).

Add it all up, and you have the perfect storm in recent months for a sector that has produced average returns of 7pc per annum since 2013, including 6.3pc from income. Discounts have widened sharply and now run at around 35pc for the broad sector average, according to fund analysts at JP Morgan, which is very high by historical standards.

A cynic might at this point argue that all these trends could get worse. Discounts could widen as dividends are cut. There have also been some unforced management errors regarding strategic reviews in the sector, undermining investor confidence in many fund boards.

The optimist, and I’m a cautious one, thinks that most of the bad news is already in the price, especially if you select your fund carefully. 

The policy regime is unlikely to change until the next general election, and I’m one of the few optimists who think UK gilt rates might possibly have overshot on the way up. Who knows? The Iran crisis might even help with higher energy prices in the medium term.

Nevertheless, even I’d still be cautious at this stage and opt for funds that have more conservative balance sheets and assets at scale in the right places.

Two names jump into my mind: one from a related but distinct sector, Greencoat UK Wind (UKW), and the other, a more diversified infrastructure fund, International Public Partnerships (INPP).

The first, Greencoat UK Wind, is the biggest fund in the space and boasts a progressive dividend policy backed by £1bn of free cash flow over the next five years. 

Power prices could drop to as little as £30 per megawatt hour before the dividend is uncovered, and the 42.5pc gearing seems manageable, although there is a chunk of debt coming up for refinancing in 2027.

Its discount is the lowest in the sector at around 29pc according to JP Morgan, and its dividend is an attractive 10.8pc.

JP Morgan analysts recently said: “We think UKW offers a reasonable hedge for investors concerned about the possibility of higher power prices and inflation.” I’d agree.

By contrast, INPP is a broadly diversified infrastructure investor that has given careful thought to the growth problem. It’s invested heavily in two crucial pieces of energy infrastructure: new transmission cables, which are essential for handling intermittent and geographically uneven output, and nuclear power.

The transmission assets are worth around a fifth of the portfolio, while the nuclear assets consist of a £254m commitment to Sizewell C.

Last week’s robust trading numbers showed a diversified £2.2bn fund that has delivered just under 100pc NAV growth in the last 10 years, on a well-backed dividend of 6.8pc.

Regardless of whether you are a net zero cynic, more intermittent renewable energy is going to surge into the system during the next few years and energy prices will remain high, which should benefit Greencoat UK Wind – which has lots of valuable onshore wind assets.

That means more transmission spending and more base-load nuclear, which should count in INPP’s favour.

data.title: Jim Ratcliffe backs Tory plans to scrap net zero taxes
data.description:
Sir Jim Ratcliffe has backed Conservative plans to scrap net zero taxes in a move that would slash energy bills for households and businesses.

The billionaire founder of petrochemicals giant Ineos said he welcomed plans by the Conservative Party to completely scrap levies on carbon emissions if it wins the next election.

Sir Jim said he was in favour of policies that would “keep the lights on” and make energy affordable and green “in a way that is practical and sustainable”.

He previously warned that carbon taxes were hurting European industry because they made domestic products far more expensive than those coming from countries such as China.

Unveiling the plans to axe levies on carbon emissions, Kemi Badenoch, the Conservative leader, said they were “killing British industry and fatally weakening our national resilience”.

Under the Conservatives’ proposals, industrial firms would no longer have to buy so-called carbon credits to compensate for emissions generated under the Emissions Trading Scheme (ETS).

The changes would also make electricity cheaper for all consumers by cutting the cost of gas-fired power generation by around one third.

The proposals are supported by major industrial companies, including oil giant ExxonMobil and chemicals producer Huntsman Corporation, as well as industry bodies such as the Chemical Industries Association and Ceramics UK.

However, the plans are likely to provoke outrage among climate campaigners who claim that carbon taxes are essential to deterring investment in fossil fuel projects that cause global warming.

It would also be likely to put the UK at odds with the European Union, which is introducing a “carbon border” mechanism.

Mrs Badenoch said: “I have heard from countless bosses how carbon taxes and green levies have made doing business in Britain much, much harder than it needs to be.

“It’s time to reverse decades of deindustrialisation by doing what Keir Starmer lacks the backbone to do: axe the carbon tax in its entirety.

“We all want to leave a better environment for the next generation, but it is madness to pursue that goal by killing British industry and fatally weakening our national resilience.”

Paul Greenwood, chairman of ExxonMobil’s UK business, which runs the Fawley refinery in Hampshire, said his company “pays hundreds of millions of pounds in CO2 costs every year that many of our international competitors do not pay”.

He said: “We support policies that create a level playing field and allow us to continue building the strong refineries on which this country depends.

“Continuing as-is risks both jobs and our national security.”

Peter Huntsman, president of Huntsman Corporation, which runs the at-risk Wilton aniline plant in Teesside, said: “The carbon tax, along with banning domestic energy production, is driving the deindustrialisation of Britain, pushing up costs, weakening competitiveness and destroying jobs and livelihoods.”

Green backlash

However, Doug Parr, chief scientist at Greenpeace UK, warned against the proposals, saying: “You can construct carbon taxes in different ways that meet your policy goals, including by returning money to taxpayers through dividends.

“The question for the Conservatives now is how are they going to fill the hole in tax revenues that this would create, and, more importantly, if they say they believe in climate change and scientists are correct that it is one of the biggest threats to our future, then what are they actually going to do about it?

“At the moment, they are stripping away all the policy measures that would tackle the problem.”

Mandatory carbon taxes were first introduced under the coalition government led by David Cameron, the Conservative prime minister, in 2013 via the Carbon Price Support (CPS).

It was designed to encourage the phase-out of coal-fired power generation, but also applies to gas-fired plants. Following the closure of Britain’s last coal power plant in September 2024, some experts have now called for the CPS’s abolition.

According to the climate think tank Ember, the average cost of gas-fired power generation was £80.62 per megawatt-hour in February. Of this, it said £25.53 was carbon taxes.

This is significant because gas plants set electricity prices for all generators more than 90pc of the time, meaning it inflates the cost British consumers pay for a range of types of power.

The emissions trading scheme, meanwhile, has been in place since 2021 and allocates carbon credits to industrial businesses that generate emissions.

If they generate emissions beyond the amount of credits they have, they must buy more from others on the market.

The carbon allowances are then tightened each year to encourage more firms to adopt carbon-free manufacturing processes.

Trade bodies such as British Glass and Ceramics UK have warned that many green technologies needed to decarbonise their industries are not yet mature or cost-effective.

It means, they say, that companies are effectively doomed to pay crippling taxes that have made them uncompetitive compared to foreign rivals.

The Chemical Industries Association has also blamed carbon taxes for a spate of plant closures in recent years.

Chris McDonald MP, Labour’s Industry Minister, said: “Kemi Badenoch has exposed her own hypocrisy given she personally introduced these measures as a Tory Treasury minister.

“It’s a total embarrassment for her to, yet again, be railing against her own work in government. Her new pledge is wrong, and it would hammer industry.

“This multibillion-pound unfunded spending commitment has echoes of Liz Truss and would leave working people picking up the bill. The failed Conservative Party haven’t changed, and their sums still don’t add up.

“Through our Industrial Strategy, Labour is slashing electricity costs for business by 25pc. Our long-term plan, in partnership with industry, will boost investment, create good skilled jobs, and make Britain the best place to do business.”

First, they increase the risk-free rates built into the valuation models, and secondly, they make the dividends paid out by many funds less attractive, especially if you can get, say, 5pc for lending to the Government long term and risk-free (although fans of the sector might argue that gilt yields are fixed whereas many fund dividends might grow with inflation).

Add it all up, and you have the perfect storm in recent months for a sector that has produced average returns of 7pc per annum since 2013, including 6.3pc from income. Discounts have widened sharply and now run at around 35pc for the broad sector average, according to fund analysts at JP Morgan, which is very high by historical standards.

A cynic might at this point argue that all these trends could get worse. Discounts could widen as dividends are cut. There have also been some unforced management errors regarding strategic reviews in the sector, undermining investor confidence in many fund boards.

The optimist, and I’m a cautious one, thinks that most of the bad news is already in the price, especially if you select your fund carefully. 

The policy regime is unlikely to change until the next general election, and I’m one of the few optimists who think UK gilt rates might possibly have overshot on the way up. Who knows? The Iran crisis might even help with higher energy prices in the medium term.

Nevertheless, even I’d still be cautious at this stage and opt for funds that have more conservative balance sheets and assets at scale in the right places.

Two names jump into my mind: one from a related but distinct sector, Greencoat UK Wind (UKW), and the other, a more diversified infrastructure fund, International Public Partnerships (INPP).

The first, Greencoat UK Wind, is the biggest fund in the space and boasts a progressive dividend policy backed by £1bn of free cash flow over the next five years. 

Power prices could drop to as little as £30 per megawatt hour before the dividend is uncovered, and the 42.5pc gearing seems manageable, although there is a chunk of debt coming up for refinancing in 2027.

Its discount is the lowest in the sector at around 29pc according to JP Morgan, and its dividend is an attractive 10.8pc.

JP Morgan analysts recently said: “We think UKW offers a reasonable hedge for investors concerned about the possibility of higher power prices and inflation.” I’d agree.

By contrast, INPP is a broadly diversified infrastructure investor that has given careful thought to the growth problem. It’s invested heavily in two crucial pieces of energy infrastructure: new transmission cables, which are essential for handling intermittent and geographically uneven output, and nuclear power.

The transmission assets are worth around a fifth of the portfolio, while the nuclear assets consist of a £254m commitment to Sizewell C.

Last week’s robust trading numbers showed a diversified £2.2bn fund that has delivered just under 100pc NAV growth in the last 10 years, on a well-backed dividend of 6.8pc.

Regardless of whether you are a net zero cynic, more intermittent renewable energy is going to surge into the system during the next few years and energy prices will remain high, which should benefit Greencoat UK Wind – which has lots of valuable onshore wind assets.

That means more transmission spending and more base-load nuclear, which should count in INPP’s favour.

data.title: Making bombs won’t save Volkswagen
data.description:
It summons up dark memories. It may prove ethically troubling, both for its workforce and for its local community.

As reports emerge that Volkswagen may start making parts for the Israeli Iron Dome, lots of difficult questions are being asked about the pivot from making cars to manufacturing military equipment.

However, there is a more serious issue, both for VW and for the rest of Europe’s struggling auto industry.

What makes them think they will be any good at it?

Defence is a high-tech, fiercely competitive industry and not one that anyone can easily conquer.

VW certainly needs to try something. What was once the biggest auto company in the world and the emblem of Germany’s post-war economic miracle is in deep trouble.

Soaring energy costs in Germany have been a huge burden alongside the European Union’s blundering determination to impose a ban on new petrol and diesel cars in the 2030s.

Over the last five years, VW’s share price has fallen by more than 60pc and it has started to cut its workforce. Its net profits fell by 44pc last year. The company had planned to become a major manufacturer of electric vehicles but the new Chinese players appear to have that market sewn up with cheaper – and often better – models. The outlook is grim.

Its solution appears to be a pivot to the defence industry.

While VW has publicly ruled out making weapons, reports emerged this week that its Osnabrück plant, currently threatened with closure, may start making parts for the Iron Dome anti-missile system produced by Israel’s Rafael Advanced Defence Systems.

There were reports last year that Rheinmetall, Germany’s largest defence company, could take over the plant instead. We will see in the next few weeks what happens.

On one level, switching from cars to tanks and guns might make a lot of sense. Defence is booming, especially in Germany where the government is spending tens of billions of euros on re-equipping its armed forces. With the war in Iran, demand is only going to grow in the next few years.

Every government in the world will be looking at installing anti-missile shields – apart from the UK, where we will probably spend the money on extra welfare instead – as well as re-stocking arsenals that are already looking badly depleted.

Who knows, VW might set a template for the entire industry with the likes of BMW, Stellantis (the owner of Fiat and Citroën) and Renault all moving into defence manufacturing as well.

Even Britain’s Jaguar Land Rover could opt to go back to its roots – at least for its Land Rover unit – and start making military off-roaders instead of plush SUVs for the school run in the world’s more prosperous suburbs. It could be the saviour of an industry that looks as if it is heading into terminal trouble.

Here’s the problem, however. It will be a lot harder than it looks.

There may be some superficial similarities. An auto plant needs to bash a lot of metal, just as a defence factory does. It has to keep costs down and the lines rolling, and it needs to be able to put together a lot of complex parts very efficiently.

In a modest way, the auto manufacturers might be able to make a success of it, at least as subcontractors of the major established giants.

Even so, let’s be honest here. This is not the Second World War, when every available scrap of industrial capacity had to be converted to armaments manufacturing to keep the front lines supplied and every production site from bicycle factories to food plants suddenly found themselves making bullets and bombs.

Modern defence manufacturing involves a very different skill-set. It is high-precision and high-tech, and it involves vast quantities of research and development, as well as massive investments in a skilled workforce.

Just take a look at the world’s top defence manufacturers.

The top three are all American, led by Lockheed Martin, RTX and Northrop Grumman, with Britain’s BAE Systems coming in at number four and Italy’s Leonardo at number 11 in the global market.

These are all companies with long and deep histories in the industry, with established contracts and with relationships with the military that stretch back for generations. They spend billions of dollars developing the best kit that can be made and testing it in the most extreme conditions.

The idea that VW or any of the other traditional auto manufacturers can suddenly break into that list is laughable. It is not going to happen. They don’t have the skills or the expertise, nor are the production lines set up in the right way.

Even if they were, it is a fiercely competitive industry, with their customers largely national governments who are going to spend their budgets on domestic manufacturers instead of in other countries.

If there is space for new players, they are far more likely to be from Ukraine – where local manufacturers have been forced to become world leaders in cheap, effective drones – or from Israel – where they have been forced to become experts in missile protection – than from Germany, France or indeed Britain. It isn’t going to happen.

Europe’s traditional auto giants certainly need to do something. With each month that passes, it becomes increasingly clear that attempts to replace petrol cars with battery-powered ones has been catastrophically mismanaged.

Bureaucrats and politicians appear to care more about grandstanding to the green lobby than preserving vital jobs and manufacturing capacity. It is hard to see how any of them are going to survive the Chinese onslaught on the industry.

Even so, these companies are kidding themselves if they think that switching to making bullets and bombs will save them.

It won’t. All it will do is guarantee terminal decline.

data.title: Ofcom under fire for handing £50,000 to The Guardian’s charity
data.description:
Broadcasting watchdog Ofcom has come under fire after handing £50,000 to The Guardian’s charity.

Filings show Ofcom provided £50,000 in funding to The Guardian Foundation last year to support its Untold Stories project, which teaches news and media literacy skills to children in impoverished areas of Greater Manchester and London.

In addition to outreach programmes, the scheme invites young participants to a dedicated classroom in The Guardian’s London headquarters to learn about news reporting.

Ofcom regulates TV and radio, rather than the newspaper industry. However, its decision to channel funding to a charity linked to a news outlet has sparked controversy.

Nigel Farage, leader of Reform UK and a presenter on GB News, said: “This is exactly the sort of cosy media club that GB News seeks to disrupt. Payments like this continue to raise questions about Ofcom’s role in the media landscape.”

Ofcom said the Untold Stories scheme was part of its Making Sense of Media programme, which forms part of its statutory duties to support media literacy. The two-year contract was won by The Guardian Foundation following a tender process last year.

But Lord Young of Acton, the Tory peer and founder of the Free Speech Union, said the payment was particularly concerning given The Guardian is not a member of the press regulatory Ipso, instead policing complaints internally.

He said: “It’s genuinely odd that Ofcom has given £50,000 to The Guardian when, almost alone among UK newspapers, The Guardian eschews any regulation of its content.

“If you’re misrepresented by The Guardian, your only recourse is to complain to its internal ombudsman – in other words, it marks its own homework. If only Ofcom granted the same latitude to GB News and Elon Musk.”

Ofcom’s battles

Ofcom’s work has been thrust increasingly into the spotlight in recent years, especially as its new role overseeing online safety laws has put it up against US tech giants and triggered accusations of censorship.

Meanwhile, the launch of opinionated broadcaster GB News has sparked rows over free speech.

The watchdog last year lost a high-profile High Court battle against GB News over its ruling that the channel had breached rules about the use of politicians as presenters in programmes fronted by Sir Jacob Rees-Mogg.

It marked the first time Ofcom lost a legal challenge against one of its decisions on broadcasting standards. The regulator’s advisory fees jumped to £4.6m last year after it forked out for lawyers, while it was also ordered to cover GB News’s costs.

An Ofcom spokesman said: “As part of our statutory duties to improve media literacy, we commission a wide range of community and grassroots organisations through our Making Sense of Media programme. Untold Stories is one of these projects.

“The Guardian Foundation – which is separate to The Guardian’s news operation – was awarded the contract to deliver the work through a robust and competitive tender process.”

A spokesman for The Guardian Foundation said: “The Guardian Foundation’s Untold Stories project delivers resources and workshops in schools and at our education centre.

“The aim is to support young people to develop their voice, think critically and build resilience against misinformation. Led by our education experts, the project will provide vital data to inform future work across the sector.”

The Guardian Foundation was set up to promote press freedom and access to liberal journalism around the world. Its primary funder is the Scott Trust, the £1.2bn fund that owns The Guardian.

Alongside media literacy initiatives, it also provides support to independent media organisations and administers the Scott Trust Bursary scheme, which provides tuition fees for student journalists as well as paid placements and the opportunity to work at The Guardian after graduation.

data.title: A shadow banking meltdown would spell disaster for Maga
data.description:
Blue Owl Credit, one of the leading players in the private credit industry, has already started limiting investors’ ability to withdraw their money.

The shares of asset managers have already tanked on fears of contagion. And US Treasury officials have started calling meetings to assess the scale of potential losses.

It remains to be seen whether the wobbles within the “shadow banking” industry trigger a full-scale panic. Even so, one point is surely already clear: a crash would be a catastrophe for Donald Trump.

Not only because of the obvious economic impact but because his whole Maga movement was built on not bailing out Wall Street.

The private credit, or shadow banking, crisis is getting more worrying all the time.

The alternative asset managers have built up huge businesses by making high-interest loans to companies. As some of those turn sour, as risky loans eventually do, the whole industry will run into trouble.

Only this week, Blue Owl Capital said it would block some redemptions from two of its major funds after it was overwhelmed by demands from its financial backers to withdraw their cash.

Plenty of other funds, such as KKR, Ares Management and Apollo Global, have limited withdrawals to 5pc, while Andrew Bailey, the Governor of the Bank of England, warned on Wednesday that private credit was “fairly opaque”.

Crucially, he also said that if investors began to suspect problems were more widespread than previously thought, confidence in the whole sector could collapse very quickly.

The scale of the potential losses is substantial. The industry is estimated to be worth between £1tn and £3tn, depending on how you count the figures, and part of the problem is that it is so lightly regulated that no one knows how much money is at stake.

It has drawn in huge sums of money and lent it out again across the world, and not least here in Britain. Of course, it remains to be seen how that plays out.

The private credit firms may find that while they suffer some losses, enough of the loans will be solid to remain profitable, and financially viable, overall. If so, the whole “crisis” will blow over. Let’s hope so, at least.

Here’s the problem, however.

If the collapse escalates, how will the White House, the Treasury and, indeed, the Federal Reserve respond?

In normal times, we would expect the financial authorities to step in with some kind of rescue package. After all, that is what happened in the past.

After the financial crash of 2008 and 2009, a massive bailout was organised to rescue the banks from the losses they had suffered, and in the smaller bank run of 2023, when Silicon Valley Bank was in trouble, another rescue was quickly put together.

So if an asset manager faces collapse, it will get help, right?

Well, perhaps. The trouble is that Trump’s Maga movement has made it clear it does not believe in bailouts. Most of its leading figures, such as JD Vance, the US vice president, have been fiercely critical in the past, arguing that the government is there to help Main Street – and not Wall Street.

Indeed, back when Mr Vance was still a senator, he introduced “The Bank Failure Prevention Act” and teamed up with Elizabeth Warren, a US senator, to pass legislation to claw back bonuses from failed financial institutions.

Likewise, Marjorie Taylor Greene, a former US representative, another leading Maga figure who has recently fallen out with Trump, was among the harshest critics of the rescue of Silicon Valley Bank. “The fools running the bank were woke and almost became broke,” she said at the time.

And, of course, the Tea Party movement, the forerunners of Maga, had its origins in opposing the 2008 to 2009 bailouts.

In fairness, Trump himself, as so often, has been more vague about what he thinks, supporting the bailouts when he first ran for office in 2016, then switching to opposition as he figured out it played better with his base.

But no one can escape the overall picture. Insofar as Maga has a coherent world-view, admittedly a fairly questionable assumption, then surely it is on the side of small businesses, and the ordinary working man, and not the sleek financial engineers in the markets?

Against that backdrop, the White House is going to find it very hard to start writing out cheques for billions of dollars to rescue a private credit fund that is about to collapse.

The Maga movement was also opposed to getting involved in Middle Eastern wars, and it has been betrayed over that, to the understandable fury of many of its leaders.

It will be furious if it has to rescue the bankers as well.

And yet, if the shadow banking industry fails, it will take the economy down with it.

The loans at stake have been advanced to manufacturers, distributors, retailers and leisure chains, and if they all get called in, those companies will go bust and hundreds of thousands of jobs will be lost.

The rhetoric of letting the Wall Street fat cats go broke sounds fine on the campaign trail or on the floor of the Senate. It is a lot harder once you are actually in power.

If this crisis gets any worse over the coming months, Trump will have to make a fateful choice.

He will have to abandon his base. Or else he will have to let the shadow banking industry collapse, and risk a major recession.

Which way will he jump? The president is so unpredictable that it is impossible to say with any certainty. And yet one point is clear. Either way, he will be in deep political trouble.

A shadow banking crisis will destroy the financiers and their investors, but as it starts to unravel, it may well destroy the Trump presidency as well.
