data.title: Jim Ratcliffe’s chemical plant suffers £515m loss
data.description:
Sir Jim Ratcliffe’s chemicals empire racked up €593m (£515m) of losses last year, as it warned of further pain from the war in Iran.

Ineos blamed soaring energy costs and Donald Trump’s tariffs as losses jumped from €71.1m the year prior, highlighting mounting economic strain on the billionaire’s sprawling business operations.

As well as a lack of profit, Ineos’s debt pile also rose from €10.6bn to €11.7bn at the end of December 2025.

It comes after S&P Global and Fitch, two of the world’s largest rating agencies, downgraded chunks of Ineos debt in recent months.

The latest results predate the outbreak of war in Iran, with bosses warning that the conflict risks piling more pressure on the debt-laden business.

An Ineos spokesman said: “While we do not have operations in the Middle East and revenue generated in this region is not material to the [Ineos] Group, the length, impact and outcome of the ongoing military conflict in the Middle East is highly unpredictable and there can be no assurances that further unforeseen events related to this conflict will not have a further material adverse affect on our operations in the future.”

The latest results emerged as Ineos warned investors of possible delays to its €4bn (£3.5bn) flagship petrochemicals plant because of the closure of the Strait of Hormuz.

Ineos Group told investors it was facing potential problems completing its Project One plant in Antwerp, Belgium, after two of its components were stranded in the Gulf.

The plant, which will make materials for plastic products and was set to be finished by the end of this year, was already suffering from delays after its environmental permit was suspended in 2023 by the Belgian government.

Ineos has described the €4bn Project One development as the largest investment in the European chemical sector in a generation. It said last June that the plant was 70pc completed.

Project One is made up of heavy modules which have been built across Thailand, the United Arab Emirates and the Philippines before being shipped to Antwerp.

Two of those shipments are stuck in a port in the Gulf.

Iran has effectively closed the Strait of Hormuz since Israel and the US first launched strikes on the country in late February.

Ineos said that rerouting tankers around the Cape of Good Hope, away from escalating tensions in the Middle East, had already cost the business more than €30m over the last two years.

S&P revealed its latest downgrade for Ineos’s debt last month.

It warned that Ineos was facing turmoil from “challenging trading conditions” around the world for the petrochemical sector. At one point, the company was reportedly considering asset sales as it looked to refinance some of its debt pile.

Sir Jim has blamed mounting losses at his Grangemouth chemical works on government carbon taxes and Britain’s sky-high energy costs.

However, he has also triggered controversy over his comments surrounding immigration.

The Manchester United co-owner, one of Britain’s richest men with a £17bn fortune, said earlier this year that Britain was being “colonised by immigrants”. He later apologised for “offending some people” with his language.

Ineos declined to comment.

data.title: The Scott Mills fiasco is the latest proof the BBC has no idea how to manage talent
data.description:
The latest BBC talent debacle, this time involving Scott Mills, may look to some like a one-off piece of awkward editorial housekeeping. But it is increasingly difficult to see these moments as isolated incidents when they recur with such wearying regularity. Step back from the immediate noise and a broader truth comes into focus: the BBC does not primarily have a talent problem, it has a talent management problem – and it has done for years.

The list of names is now long enough to make that argument impossible to dismiss. Those who have exited amid storms of controversy include Jeremy Clarkson, Jonathan Ross, Danny Baker, Gary Lineker and Jermaine Jenas.

Then there is the vile category all of its own, occupied by Jimmy Savile, Stuart Hall and Rolf Harris, alongside Huw Edwards. These are cases of a morally different order and should never be casually mixed in with matters of contract handling, editorial judgment or career stewardship.

But set all those appalling examples to one side, and there still remains a substantial roster of presenters and performers whose treatment by the BBC has ranged from clumsy to short-sighted to downright wasteful.

Think of Tony Blackburn, Miriam O’Reilly, Paul Gambaccini and, to a lesser extent, Ken Bruce, Simon Mayo and Steve Wright, all figures whose relationships with the corporation were poorly handled or prey to the sort of institutional weather that blows through Broadcasting House whenever somebody feels the urge to rearrange the furniture.

Too often, moving talent around appears to be treated as a way of demonstrating dynamism. It allows a newly installed executive to look decisive, and it costs little in budgetary terms. What it can cost, however, is trust, loyalty, audience connection and the goodwill of the very people upon whom the BBC’s public identity depends.

That is because the BBC has never fully come to terms with what it actually is. For all its complexity, it remains at heart a talent organisation. It is built on the shoulders of individuals whose voices, faces, judgments, rhythms and quirks become familiar to millions.

Audiences do not form relationships with management structures, strategy documents or internal memos. They form relationships with people. And those people are not standard employees. They are performers, communicators, broadcasters, interpreters of mood, tone and public feeling.

They are often mavericks, sometimes difficult, usually sensitive, frequently edgy, and almost always operating under levels of scrutiny that most of the members of the organisation around them would find intolerable if it were applied to their own daily work.

That difference matters. You can tell whether on-air talent has had a good day at work because you can hear it or see it for yourself. Their failures are public, their triumphs are public, and the judgment upon them is immediate. The teams who support them are often excellent, but they are not exposed to that same relentless test. It follows, surely, that people doing this kind of work require a different kind of management. Not mollycoddling, not a free pass for bad behaviour, but a more intelligent, more structured and more informed kind of stewardship.

That has long been my view, and it is not a theory formed from the cheap seats. My first BBC Network contract came as Barry Norman’s reporter, and from there I went on to work alongside a range of remarkable BBC talent, including Anne Gregg, Anne Robinson, Jeremy Vine, Angela Rippon (and, indeed, Huw Edwards).

I spent years as on-air talent myself, albeit without quite the star wattage of some of those names, and I came to understand at close quarters the peculiar demands of the job: the scrutiny, the pressure, the emotional wear and tear, the often blurred line between the public role and the private self, and the way in which talent can flourish brilliantly in the right environment or fray in the wrong one.

Alongside that, I also spent 25 years running my own agency, Excellent Talent, representing and curating talent. The likes of Harry Enfield, Bob Harris, Johnnie Walker, Helen Lederer, Christopher Timothy, Brian Perkins and James Cosmo were all stars on our books. Looking after talent is not the same as managing any other category of staff. The skill lies in recognising that difference without becoming overprotective or indulgent, and in building structures that support both creativity and accountability.

In 2016 I wrote to then director-general, Tony Hall, suggesting that the BBC should create an internal talent agency within itself, to nurture and understand talent, and deal with the mercurial people that often occupy these roles.

What would an internal BBC talent agency have done in practice with some of the examples I mention above? It would not have waved a wand and abolished scandal, stupidity or wrongdoing. No serious person could claim that. But it could have created a form of specialist stewardship; it might have made a substantial difference to the outcomes, or at the very least to the amount of collateral damage.

Take Paul Gambaccini. After he was arrested over an unfounded abuse claim in 2013, he endured an extraordinary period of uncertainty while his reputation was shredded by suspicion before any charge was ever brought. He has said that during that time the BBC made him feel like a “non person”.

An internal talent agency would not, of course, have interfered with a police investigation, but it could have ensured that from the moment he was caught up in that nightmare, he had a dedicated internal advocate who understood both the legal sensitivity and the devastating reputational exposure involved in being a recognisable BBC voice under that kind of cloud.

It could have co-ordinated legal liaison, communications advice, mental health support, internal decision-making and, crucially, protected the presumption of innocence from being trampled inside the building by fear and optics. In Gambaccini’s case, the BBC looked more like an institution trying to distance itself from risk than one trying to handle a valued broadcaster with fairness.

The same principle applies, although differently, to Miriam O’Reilly, dropped from Countryfile in 2009, in an act of age discrimination (she successfully sued, with this argument). There, the issue was not criminal suspicion but the mishandling of a visible presenter.

It is a good example of where a proper talent agency could have made a material difference. That dispute did not simply erupt overnight. It reflected a failure of internal communication, expectation setting and the handling of perceived career transition.

Someone specialising in talent management would have seen that as exactly the sort of situation requiring early intervention: honest conversations about editorial direction, changes in role, age sensitivity, future opportunities and how those discussions were framed.

Too often in large institutions, those matters are handled clumsily by layers of management who neither understand the performer’s sense of professional identity nor appreciate how quickly bad handling can turn into grievance, humiliation and litigation. An internal agency would have been there to broker, challenge and reality-test those conversations before they hardened into a public dispute.

Ken Bruce is perhaps the most obvious example of pure mishandling rather than scandal; he was asked to leave for his new job at Greatest Hits Radio 17 days earlier than first planned. Here was one of the most successful and trusted voices in British radio, yet his departure was managed in a way that felt oddly graceless and needlessly bruising.

A proper internal talent agency would have understood that someone like Bruce was not simply a presenter occupying a slot in a schedule, but a long-term asset whose exit needed to be managed with care.

That means honest conversations early, sensible planning around succession, dignity in departure and an understanding that how you treat one star is watched carefully by every other star in the building. Instead, the handling created precisely the kind of noise and bad feeling that better stewardship should avoid. Even when talent is leaving, management matters.

The BBC has spent years behaving as though talent were both essential and faintly embarrassing, celebrated when delivering ratings and attention but regarded with suspicion the moment that same prominence creates complexity. That is not management. That is institutional ambivalence dressed up as professionalism. And it is one reason why the corporation keeps finding itself in the same muddle, dealing with the fallout of mishandled relationships, inconsistent standards, reactive decision making and a chronic failure to distinguish between strong management and bureaucratic box-ticking.

When I wrote back in 2016, I suggested I would be happy to run the internal talent agency – but received no reply. I would make that case again now to the incoming director-general Matt Brittin with even greater conviction, because the evidence has only mounted in the years since.

Because until the BBC starts managing talent as though it truly understands the peculiar, exposed, high-wire nature of the work, it will go on repeating the same mistakes, wasting the same gifts and learning the same lessons far too late. And for an organisation built so heavily on the relationship between audience and performer, that is not a minor operational weakness. It is a failure to understand the very thing that made the BBC powerful in the first place.

Jon Briggs is a former BBC Radio 5 and Radio 2 presenter, original UK voice of Siri and The Weakest Link. The first episode of his new podcast, The Price of Fame, features Ken Bruce.

data.title: Best cash Isas: Today’s latest rates
data.description:
A cash Isa is similar to a savings account, but with a crucial difference – you don’t pay tax on the interest you earn.

It is one of four types of adult individual savings accounts (Isas) – the others are stocks and shares Isas, innovative finance Isas and lifetime Isas.

You can currently save up to £20,000 per tax year, which is known as the Isa allowance. In the 2025 Budget, Chancellor Rachel Reeves announced plans to reduce this to £12,000 from April 2027 for savers under the age of 65; younger savers will have to use an investment Isa if they wish to use the remaining £8,000 of their allowance.

You can either deposit this sum into one account or spread it across several, and you can transfer your Isa savings between providers if you want to take advantage of a better rate elsewhere.

In this guide, Telegraph Money explains how cash Isas work, covering:

  • How we determine the best rates
  • The best cash Isas in April
  • Expert opinion: Things to consider when choosing a cash Isa
  • Why choose a cash Isa?
  • Cash Isa FAQs

How we determine the best rates

The Best Buy tables below show the best savings rates widely available in the market. This means certain accounts are excluded, including those that are available only to local or existing customers.

The data in these tables is provided by Savings Data Limited and is compiled using automated tracker tools and updates from savings providers. Savings Data Limited then manually checks the information and enters it into a database that feeds the live tables, which update daily.

The savings accounts shown are protected by the Financial Services Compensation Scheme (FSCS). The information in this article is intended for information purposes and should not be taken as endorsement or advice.

The best cash Isas in April 2026

Find the best cash Isa for you using the tables below.

Best easy-access cash Isas

These accounts usually allow you to make as many deposits and withdrawals as you like – but this flexibility can come with lower rates. For the savings equivalent, you can see our guide to the best easy-access savings accounts.

Best variable rate cash Isas

Accounts in the table below show the best Isa rates on the market for accounts that offer a variable rate of interest. While this could increase at any time – which can be valuable when rates are on an upward trend – it could also decrease.

Best notice cash Isas

These accounts require you to give a certain amount of notice to your provider when you want to make a withdrawal, indicating the number of days you must wait until your money is released. You can usually make as many withdrawals as you like – but be sure to check the account terms first.

If this way of saving suits you, make sure you check our guide to the best notice accounts for alternative options.

Best fixed-rate cash Isas

These Isas require you to lock your money away for a specified period of time. The rate of interest is guaranteed throughout this period, which can give you peace of mind should other rates take a tumble.

For the savings account equivalent, which you might want to use if you’ve used up your Isa allowance, see our guide to the best fixed-rate bonds.

For other types of tax-free saving, see our guide to the best Junior cash Isas for under-18s. Or, if you’re saving to buy a home or fund your retirement, don’t miss our guide to the best lifetime Isas.

Expert opinion: Things to consider when choosing a cash Isa

Choosing the best cash Isa for you can be tricky, as it depends on why you’re saving and how you want to save – but there should be options to suit everyone.

Caitlyn Eastell, of analyst Moneyfactscompare, said: “For savers that are more tax savvy, cash Isas may be the ideal option as you receive a £20,000 Isa allowance each year and can pay into multiple Isas within the same period providing this limit is not exceeded. Isas aren’t too dissimilar to their traditional savings counterparts with easy access, notice, fixed and regular accounts being an option.

“It is worth considering that for the variable rate options, interest could increase or decrease over time, and those Isas that are fixed for an agreed term will either not allow earlier access or may deduct a certain amount of interest.”

Cash Isas can also be an effective way of saving for the longer term.

Mark Hicks, of Hargreaves Lansdown, said: “Cash Isas have certainly seen a resurgence of late. Looking at what may be coming in Labour’s Budget, and the rhetoric around higher taxes, cash Isas should be the first port of call for all savers at the moment.

“If you’ve got £20,000 or less, definitely go for a cash Isa first and ensure your money is protected from tax. If you’re fortunate enough to have more than £20,000 to save in an Isa, then you can look at other saving accounts as well.”

Why choose a cash Isa?

Cash Isas are beneficial for many savers as they offer an easy way to make sure your savings interest is sheltered from income tax.

Their popularity took a dip when the personal savings allowance (PSA) was introduced in 2016, as it means some savers can earn up to £1,000 in tax-free savings interest each tax year. When interest rates are low, only those with large savings pots or high incomes come into the scope of paying tax on savings interest.

However, savings rates have been high for some time; until recently it was not uncommon to be earning 5pc. Someone with a £1,000 PSA could face a savings tax bill with £20,000 in savings – a problem that doesn’t occur with a cash Isa. What’s more, your growth is protected as it compounds for years into the future, which has helped some savers become Isa millionaires.

The main issue that comes with a cash Isa is the £20,000 Isa limit on how much you can pay into it each tax year. If you want to move a large savings deposit in order to protect your returns from tax, it may need to be moved gradually over several tax years.

In some cases, interest rates offered by cash Isas can be lower than their savings equivalent. Providers are well aware of the extra tax benefit Isas offer, and know they don’t have to be as competitive with rates.

It’s important to shop around and find the most competitive rate you can. Cash Isa rates can be lower than their savings account counterparts, but the tax-free benefits of an Isa will often outweigh the loss of a little interest.

If you’re not sure whether you could be in danger of having to pay tax on your savings interest, try the Telegraph Money savings tax calculator – simply enter your savings, interest rate and tax band and it can tell you whether you’d be better off getting an Isa.

Cash Isa FAQs

There is no limit to the number of cash Isas you can have – but it’s a good idea not to have so many that you lose track of them.

You can hold cash Isas you’ve paid into in past tax years, and – now that Isa rules have changed – you can also open and pay into as many as you like in the same tax year. The key thing is to make sure you don’t pay in more than £20,000 across all Isas in the same tax year.

Yes, you can combine Isas into one account – but in order to do this without affecting the tax status of the money, or your Isa allowance, you must move the cash via an Isa transfer.

There are quite a few rules to bear in mind when you do this – you can find out more in our comprehensive guide to Isa transfers.

This depends on the rate you’re earning and the rate of inflation. At the time of writing, lots of cash Isas can beat inflation, but you might need to shop around for them. It’s a good idea to keep an eye on what’s happening with inflation versus the best Isa rates so you can gauge whether or not you have a competitive deal.

Our inflation calculator shows the effect rising prices can have on your savings.

It’s never too late to put money in an Isa to benefit from its tax-free status. However, if you’re planning to deposit money very close to the end of the tax year on April 5, there’s a chance you could miss the deadline.

If this happens, you can still save the money – it will just be taken from the following year’s allowance instead.

The annual Isa allowance resets at the start of a tax year on April 6. You can choose to put this entire amount into one account, or split it among several.

Mr Hicks said: “Isas were typically seasonal, with a big spike in demand around the end of the tax year. They are much less timely now and every year the cash Isa season gets longer and longer.”

data.title: Gail’s in talks to stock The Observer after Guardian anti-Semitism row
data.description:
The Observer may be stocked in Gail’s as it nears a new partnership with the cafe chain following an anti-Semitism row at its former sister title.

Both sides are in advanced discussions about a distribution deal that, if confirmed, would see the Sunday newspaper sold alongside the upmarket chain’s baked goods, sandwiches and coffee across more than 200 branches.

If confirmed, the agreement would enable The Observer to reach more readers in its key demographic.

Gail’s is seen as an attractive target for The Observer because of its popularity in affluent, middle-class areas.

The chain formed a key part of the Liberal Democrats’ 2024 election campaign, when the party targeted voters in constituencies that had a Gail’s in a strategy called “Operation Cinnamon Bun”.

However, such a move could be overshadowed by a recent anti-Semitism controversy between the coffee shop chain and The Guardian, which owned The Observer until its sale to Tortoise Media last year.

The Guardian was accused of minimising racism against Jews after a recent opinion piece that downplayed repeated vandalism of a new branch of Gail’s in Archway, north London.

Founded by an Israeli baker in the 1990s, Gail’s has been targeted by pro-Palestinian activists because its largest shareholder is Bain Capital, a private equity firm that also invests in Israeli defence and cybersecurity firms.

Jonathan Liew, the columnist who wrote the article, described the vandalism as “small acts of petty symbolism” while accusing Gail’s of “heavy-handed high-street aggression” for opening a site close to a Palestinian café.

The column prompted a furious response from staff and rebukes from politicians, while protesters gathered outside the Guardian’s London offices last month to denounce what they described as the “new hate movement”.

The Guardian has since edited the article to remove the phrase “small acts of petty symbolism”, which it said was to “avoid misunderstanding”.

Nevertheless, the controversy has rekindled accusations of anti-Semitism in the newspaper’s ranks and piled fresh pressure on Katharine Viner, the editor, to act.

While The Observer’s discussions are unrelated to the scandal, they suggest a divergence in political viewpoints between the Sunday title and its former stablemate.

James Harding, the former head of BBC News and now the head of Tortoise Media, has vowed to revive The Observer’s status as an independent voice in liberal journalism.

The newspaper, founded in 1791, launched its first digital subscription model in November. However, it is grappling with a rocky transition to the digital era and had 3,500 online subscribers as of January, according to figures provided to investors.

Around 25 people are expected to leave The Observer after it offered voluntary redundancy to all employees.

The Observer is also understood to be in conversations with other outlets alongside Gail’s about similar deals.

The newspaper and Gail’s declined to comment.

data.title: Labour appoints former Channel 4 chief as Ofcom chairman
data.description:
Labour has appointed Channel 4’s former chairman to helm Ofcom.

Sir Ian Cheshire, a City grandee, has been named as the Government’s preferred candidate to replace Tory peer Lord Grade as chairman of the media watchdog. His appointment comes as the regulator enters a new era policing social media giants.

As Channel 4 chairman, Sir Ian helped see off a government attempt to privatise the youth-focused broadcaster.

He was previously chief executive of B&Q-owner Kingfisher and has served on the boards of major companies including Barclays, Landsec and BT. He has also held several government positions, including as lead non-executive director of the Cabinet Office.

Sir Ian was picked ahead of the veteran Labour peer Baroness Hodge of Barking, who was considered the frontrunner, as well as Sir Jeremy Wright, a former Conservative culture secretary who drew up a white paper on online safety.

Labour has opted for a non-political figure to lead Ofcom following controversies over previous appointments. Paul Dacre, the former Daily Mail editor, was picked to lead the regulator by Boris Johnson in 2021 but pulled out of the race after he was rejected by an independent panel.

Sir Keir Starmer was accused of “meddling” in the latest appointment process after The Telegraph revealed that his officials demanded changes to the shortlist of candidates. Downing Street said it rejected the characterisation.

The appointment comes at a key time for Ofcom, which is under pressure to speed up enforcement of the Online Safety Act, key elements of which came into force last year.

The laws are aimed at cracking down on lax practices by social media companies and protecting people, particularly children, from harmful material online.

Ofcom has launched an investigation into Elon Musk’s X over sexualised images created by its Grok chabot. But Liz Kendall, the Technology Secretary, has warned the regulator that it must move faster or risk losing public trust.

Ofcom, which also oversees the TV and radio industries, has also come under scrutiny for its approach to regulating the Right-wing television channel GB News, while it will inform decisions about the future of the licence fee ahead of the BBC’s Charter renewal next year.

Sir Ian will replace Lord Grade, a former chairman of both the BBC and ITV, when he steps down at the end of the month. He will be paid £120,000 a year for the three-day-per-week role.

He will be subject to a pre-appointment hearing of the science, innovation and technology select committee before the decision is confirmed.

Sir Ian said: “Across my career in e-commerce, telecoms and broadcasting I have seen first-hand how much effective regulation matters – for consumers, for businesses and for the wider economy.

“Ofcom has a critical job to do at a time of rapid change in how people communicate, access information and stay safe online. I look forward to setting out my vision for how Ofcom can meet those challenges when I appear before the select committee.”

Ms Kendall said: “Sir Ian has a proven track record of leading complex organisations through periods of significant change, and that is exactly the kind of leadership Ofcom needs right now.

“I also want to pay tribute to Lord Grade for his contribution as chair. He has steered Ofcom through a period of profound change, and his leadership during the introduction of the Online Safety Act has been pivotal.”

data.title: Dithering Labour is crippling Britain’s defence renaissance
data.description:
For want of a nail, the saying goes, the battle was lost.

When a tiny defence firm goes bust, Britain falls further off the map of global power.

MPs were recently given a vivid example of how Government dithering was causing lasting damage.

MTE in Slaithwaite, West Yorkshire, was just one of thousands of engineering companies that are invisible to policymakers in Westminster. It performed specialised hardening and treatment of turbine blades but was forced to close its doors.

As Andrew Kinniburgh, the director general of members’ association Make UK Defence explained, the blades journey several times between Italy, the UK, Ireland and the US before returning here for finishing.

Small companies perform much of this work. Each closure makes the nation less attractive for business.

“The inevitable consequence of that little 30-person company going bust is that the company procuring those blades will say, ‘Actually, it is a bit easier just to do it in the States because we can get the machining done there and the British company – which is fantastic and a world leader in what it does – is just a bit of an inconvenience,’” he says.

A ripple has a “tsunami wave of impact on the supply chain”, he adds.

One reason is Ed Miliband’s green ideology. High energy costs are “slowly deindustrialising” our manufacturing capacity, says Kinniburgh. But this is not why MPs wanted to hear from industry.

The Government has created a fresh crisis by delaying crucial spending details. The defence investment plan, originally scheduled for last autumn, is eight months overdue.

“Defence is a strange market in which the core customer is [the] Government – so how [the] Government behaves has a huge impact on the sector,” says Emma Baker, an adviser for ADS Group.

Samira Braund, the company’s defence director, told MPs that the ecosystem was paralysed: “With us delaying and prevaricating, we are basically telling those companies: perhaps you should invest [in] someone else.”

This is a completely unforced error and it’s snuffing out growing optimism in the defence engineering supply chains.

With national security now a higher priority than it has been for decades, Britain has the talent and capability to take advantage of it and grow.

There are good reasons why this optimism was well-founded.

Firstly, the engineer-entrepreneur is perhaps the most underrated figure in Britain’s economic history and hasn’t gone away.

“The brainpower in our engineering is phenomenal,” says Marcus Gibson, the founder of a research agency which monitors Britain’s small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs). “We have a quality that the French and Germans can’t match.”

This is not an idle boast.

The privately held family firm of Martin-Baker, which makes ejector seats, is a global leader with 70pc of the military market.

It has achieved the seemingly impossible: beating out American defence contractors on their home turf. The US uses “military standards” to tilt the playing field in favour of domestic suppliers.

Another world-beater is Accuracy International, which has built its rifles for demanding combat conditions. It’s one of very few firms that manufacture their components in-house and even “shave their own barrels”.

With fewer than a hundred employees, these are not headline-grabbing or heavyweight firms – although with more favourable capital conditions, we could have many more of them.

The major manufacturers such as BAE and Rolls-Royce have thousands of small precision engineering shops in their supply chains.

It’s such SMEs that kept the British economy ticking along in the 1930s, says Gibson, stopping a recession from becoming a deeper depression as they found markets for products such as new electrical motors.

British engineers have other advantages too. People like doing business with British firms, I am regularly told, as they leave some margin on the table. Fair play is not a myth.

They can also improvise brilliantly, something needed more than ever in a fast-moving field such as drones and uncrewed vehicles.

Five drone firms have set up in Swindon, Wiltshire, bringing know-how from the front in the Donbas. So the dithering over spending could not have come at a worse time for industry.

“The very cautious approach to procurement that we see coming out of the Government now is really not helpful,” says Trevor Taylor, a research fellow at the Royal United Services Institute, a think tank.

“Longer term contracts can encourage investment by SMEs: six years rather than one plus one, with an invitation to recompete.”

The Ministry of Defence knows what can be fixed, but there’s a long way to go.

“If you’re an innovative company do you want to be subjected to going into a competition? There’s a risk your expertise will leak out, it’s costly and it’s time-consuming,” says Taylor.

And if you’re anointed as a sole supplier, there’s a dedicated quango set up to pore over the contracts.

Small British engineering firms such as Cheshire’s Didsbury Engineering are being snapped up by Swedish firms, a largely unreported trend. Under the “Swedish model” of capital investment, they will be in a good place: benefitting from patient long-term planning.

Although Sweden only joined Nato two years ago, it has doubled its defence budget in three years to 2.8pc of GDP and its engineering sector will reap the rewards.

Our British firms, who must sell up simply to grow the business, must look on such commitment to their craft with envy.

data.title: Housebuilder facing cash squeeze handed £300m by government
data.description:
Labour is under pressure to explain why the Government has handed a troubled housebuilder more than £300m in taxpayer-backed loans and contracts.

Paul Holmes, the shadow housing minister, said there were “questions about Labour’s judgement” after it partnered with Vistry to deliver affordable housing.

The London-listed building firm has been handed around £315m in grants and loans, according to Matthew Pennycook, the housing minister.

Homes England, the housing quango responsible for allocating the funds, does not directly monitor the financial viability of the companies it works with, he added.

Shares in Vistry have crashed by more than 50pc over the past month following the surprise departure of Greg Fitzgerald, chief executive, and amid concerns about a cash squeeze at the business.

Vistry has announced plans to offer deep discounts on some of its homes to offload them and raise capital. Reports suggest it has also demanded delays on paying subcontractors and asked for discounts as it battles to preserve cash.

It follows a string of profit warnings after bosses underestimated the cost of building homes in the south of England.

Paul Holmes, the Conservative shadow Housing minister, submitted an official parliamentary question to Mr Pennycook last week on the taxpayer’s “financial commitment to Vistry”.

“Vistry’s plight raises serious doubts about Labour’s ability to deliver their so-called Affordable Homes Programme,” Mr Holmes told The Telegraph.

“And it also raises questions about Labour’s judgement. If the Government isn’t properly scrutinising the companies it partners with, how can it say whether they will succeed or fail?

“Labour must come clean about how and why ministers concluded Vistry was the best choice – and tell us how much taxpayers’ money could be lost.”

In November, ministers cleared Vistry to bid for as much as £700m in taxpayer subsidies as part of the Government’s £39bn affordable homes programme. It has the same status under the existing programme, begun by the Conservatives in 2021.

Anthony Codling of RBC Capital Markets said there were “amber flags” about Vistry’s financial position.

“Vistry needs to slow down, shrink the business, stabilise the cash situation before it sees growth,” he said.

Mr Codling added that the going concern note in Vistry’s latest financial accounts had grown from around 350 words to more than 1,700 words, three times the size that it was this time last year, which “paints a darker downside” than before.

The wider economic backdrop also offers little comfort, with housebuilders warning of inflationary uncertainty stemming from the conflict in Iran.

“If the market really turns down, it’ll get a lot trickier for Vistry,” said Aynsley Lammin of Investec.

Mr Pennycook said that the Affordable Homes Programme does not carry hard credit risk exposure on companies it works with, as it is a grant programme, so a “purposely high-level review” is undertaken instead.

The ministers added that there are “ no checks undertaken on the off-balance sheet liabilities” as it “is not the role of Homes England to undertake liquidity tests on strategic partners”.

A Vistry spokesman said that taxpayer money was not at risk and said it “has been deployed on building affordable homes that this country desperately needs”.

They added that Vistry “continues to maintain a comfortable level of headroom against its banking facilities”.

“We work closely with Homes England on the delivery of new homes, and, like all of its partners, our programme is closely monitored and audited,” they said.

“The grant funding has all been committed, and the homes for which the funds were allocated are now contracted and owned by regulated, registered providers.”

In March, Mr Fitzgerald vowed to shrink Vistry’s debt by the end of this year.

A spokesman for Homes England said: “Homes England operates within robust, transparent frameworks when working with delivery partners. We don’t comment on the commercial or financial position of individual partners.”

The Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government declined to comment.

data.title: Heathrow’s new car parks to cost more than Gatwick’s second runway
data.description:
From HS2 to Crossrail, British infrastructure projects lead the world in one unenviable respect: their sizeable, often runaway budgets.

The bill for big-ticket construction schemes typically starts off with eye-watering sums – and then climb to even more staggering levels as budgets are blown and costs run rampant.

Now a plan to provide 36,000 parking spaces at Heathrow airport is poised to join the pantheon of cash-hungry mega-projects after it was revealed that it will come with a staggering £2.6bn price tag.

That’s £400m more than the estimated outlay for a new runway at Gatwick and more than half the cost of Heathrow’s Terminal 5 – the largest free-standing structure in the UK.

At £72,000 per parking space, the two multi-storey car parks planned under the scheme will also be comfortably the most expensive in Britain and a contender for the costliest ever built.

Heathrow argues the price per space will be lower because only half the overall cost, about £1.3bn, is for the buildings themselves, with the rest of the spending going towards associated infrastructure such as bus facilities.

Yet the airport has faced criticism from some of the world’s largest airlines, who will ultimately pick up the bill for the world’s most expensive car park.

In a week when National Car Parks (NCP), the country’s biggest car park operator, collapsed into administration, the plan might seem an ambitious one.

However, unlike NCP, which was hit by a drop in commuting, Heathrow can rely on a captive customer base and funding rules that mean construction costs are ultimately likely to be passed on as aircraft landing fees.

Airline bosses have attacked the project as typical of Heathrow’s largesse in lavishing cash on gold-plated enhancements that they and their customers must ultimately pay for.

The airport says the car parks will be a vital component of its £49bn plan for a third runway that will lift passenger numbers from 84 million a year to as many as 150 million.

It says the sites – one next to the M4 and the other to the south near the M25 – will be needed to cope with the thousands of additional passengers driving to what is already Europe’s busiest airport each day, even if a higher proportion of people take public transport.

And it argues that the expense of the project reflects construction costs in London that are themselves amongst the highest in the world.

But Luis Gallego, the boss of British Airways (BA) owner International Airlines Group, said the plan was overpriced.

He highlighted the shortcomings of a funding model that allows Heathrow to charge airlines a regulated fee to cover building and operating costs.

“We support expansion, but not at any cost. All future spending must be carefully controlled as Heathrow already charges passengers twice as much as Amsterdam and three times as much as Paris,” he said.

Willie Walsh, the director general of the International Air Transport Association, was blunter, describing the car parks plan as “madness” and saying his mind “boggled” at the £2.6bn bill.

He said Heathrow had proposed what would have been the world’s most expensive car park when he was chief executive of BA as part of a previous runway plan that was rejected in 2010.

He said: “You can find data on what it costs to build a car park, there’s lots of evidence there, and Heathrow is just way out of line with the rest of the world.

“The problem is that they are incentivised to spend as much money as possible and to build the most expensive car park and to be proud of that.”

Heathrow has said that calling the new structures car parks does not do them justice.

Thomas Woldbye, the airport’s chief executive, has instead referred to them as “parking villages”.

The blueprint for the new runway proposes “two large parkway car parks” that would replace more than 20 existing facilities, with the northern site able to accommodate 20,000 vehicles and the southern one 16,000.

It adds: “This could support fast and efficient connections to each terminal campus via dedicated transport systems.

“These new parkways could also provide local communities with improved access to airport terminals and public transport links and help reduce congestion on local roads.”

While a leaked Heathrow briefing paper states the cost of the parkways to be £2.6bn, a spokesman said the parking areas alone will consume only about half the budget, with the rest covering the cost of associated infrastructure such as bus facilities.

Major profit centre

The car parks may be necessary to cope with an influx of new passengers, but they would also be a major profit centre, with parking fees contributing £200m in revenue last year.

Heathrow’s long-stay car parks currently charge £46.80 for the first day and £37.40 for each additional 24-hour period.

Taking an average of £40 per day, the number of spaces planned could generate more than £500m in revenue over the course of a year.

That is not including income from drop-off charges and the short-stay car parks nearer to terminals that the airport has indicated it plans to retain.

Michael O’Leary, the chief executive of Ryanair, which does not fly from Heathrow, said drop-off fees were “a scandal” across UK airports and that parking had been turned into a cash cow integral to their business models.

He said: “The more they earn from car parks the more they will dividend it out to their shareholders and then complain that they can’t make a reasonable return on the risks they were taking investing in runways in London.

“But there’s no risk at all, it’s a complete freebie, which is why pension funds now own most of these airports.”

O’Leary said the cost of runways, car parks and terminals was ultimately the result of a “broken” regulatory model that “rewards inefficiency and incentivises airports to squander gazillions”.

A study from Oxford Global Projects commissioned by Heathrow Reimagined, a lobby group formed by airlines including BA, has suggested that Heathrow’s construction costs across a variety of infrastructure including car parks are consistently among the highest in the world.

The report said the cost of the runway alone – covering cement, asphalt and lights – would come in at more than £800,000 per metre, making it one of the most expensive ever built.

Jonathan Simcock, author of The Delivery Gap, an examination of failed infrastructure projects, said companies like Heathrow have an incentive to inflate estimated costs by “including all the bells and whistles they can”.

In so doing, he said, they aim to secure regulatory approval for levels of spending – in Heathrow’s case to be funded by airlines and their customers – that will cover all eventualities and “ensure they are not left holding the baby”.

“If the estimate is inflated then the company ends up making an inappropriate profit. But if it’s undercooked the company gets into trouble, somebody has to bail them out and the country doesn’t get the infrastructure it needs,” he says.

Woldbye has said that the costings for the car parks and the wider runway project could change and that Heathrow is open to conversations about modifying its plans.

However, Simcock warned: “When the conversation is about how it can possibly cost that much, very often it turns out that it should have been, can you really do it for that?

“For very large and highly complex HS2-type things – and Heathrow is big and complex – it is rare to get nice surprises and much more common to get nasty ones.”

data.title: Devon ‘war metal’ mine to help power Britain’s rearmament
data.description:
One of the largest tungsten deposits in the world is set to be mined in Devon as the Middle East crisis turbo-charges demand for the critical “war metal”.

The Hemerdon mine, near Plymouth, was discovered in 1867 and exploited through both world wars, as well as briefly from 2015 to 2018 before it was closed because of a lack of funds.

Now the site is being developed again by Tungsten West, with the wars in Ukraine and Iran triggering a surge in demand for munitions.

Tungsten is widely used in defence because of its tough, dense nature and its 3,422C melting point. The metal is used in everything from hypersonic missile nose cones to armour-piercing bullets, bunker-busting bombs and “airburst” counter-drone shells.

The price of the metal has risen sharply since China – which produces 80pc of global supplies – imposed export restrictions a year ago, prompting Western countries to seek alternate sources.

Prices also surged following the US attack on Iran in late February, as both America and Gulf countries deployed a large number of interceptors that use the metal.

Tungsten West is hoping to begin production of small amounts at Hemerdon later this year before ramping up to full capacity by 2027.

At peak production, the London-listed company estimates the site will produce around 3,300 tonnes of tungsten concentrate per year – or around 20pc of global supplies outside China.

That is enough to meet about a third of all defence-related demand globally.

Analysts have described the Devon deposits as among the largest untapped tungsten resources in the world.

Once fully up and running, the project – which will conduct mining operations for about 11 years – is expected to support more than 300 permanent jobs and generate hundreds of millions of pounds in economic value.

Nick Lawson, the boss of investment group Ocean Wall, said: “Hemerdon is one of the world’s great tungsten deposits and it sits in Devon.

“At a moment when Western supply chain vulnerability has become a strategic liability, Britain producing its own critical minerals is not an option, it is an obligation.”

Jeff Court, the chief executive of Tungsten West, said the mine represented a “very significant” chunk of the “non-China-sourced market”, adding that his customers “certainly understand the risk of being fully dependent, or highly dependent, on a China supply chain”.

“That is why they’re now looking at how to diversify,” he said.

While the mine will deliver a boost to defence, Mr Court said the mine’s primary buyers would be companies in industries such as construction, transport, manufacturing and medicine.

He added: “We’re a very good hedge and a good way to achieve supply chain resilience and support a variety of sectors, including defence, for a number of Western economies.

“As you can imagine, there’s a lot of people who were pretty keen for us to accelerate. So if we can accelerate and we can move faster, we’d like to bring forward production.”

The US has been attempting to rally allies behind initiatives to develop critical mineral supply chains that Beijing cannot strangle.

The Trump administration has also been striking deals around the world to invest in mines or processing facilities.

Mr Lawson said the search for alternatives to Chinese supplies had become “urgent” following Beijing’s decision to crack down on exports last year amid sparring over trade with Washington.

He said: “When China controls 83pc of global supply and chooses to weaponise that position, the rest of the world has no good options.”

Another tungsten project is also being developed in Cornwall by Strategic Minerals. 

The smaller Redmoor mine is undergoing detailed engineering surveys and has received £1.5m in government funding.

data.title: Marmite sold to the Americans in £50bn deal
data.description:
Marmite will fall into American hands after its owner agreed to a $66bn (£50bn) deal with a US food giant.

The brand, known for its love-it-or-hate-it slogan, is one of several supermarket staples being sold by Unilever to Baltimore-based spice and sauce maker McCormick.

Other brands changing hands under the deal include Hellmann’s mayonnaise, Knorr stock cubes, Bovril, Colman’s mustard and Pot Noodle. They will join McCormick’s stable, which includes French’s mustard and Schwartz herbs.

Fernando Fernández, the boss of Unilever, said the deal would create a “global flavour powerhouse”.

The combined company will be 65pc-owned by Unilever but will operate under McCormick’s name and leadership, with its headquarters in the US. The new company will also be listed in New York.

Unilever and McCormick said they hoped to find $600m of cost savings from the deal, a forecast sure to raise concerns about potential job losses.

Marmite was founded in Burton-on-Trent in 1902, utilising leftover yeast from a local brewery, and is still made in the Staffordshire town today. Colman’s started in Norwich in 1814 and continues to be made from mustard seeds grown in the area.

Brendan Foley, McCormick’s chairman, said a “big portion” of savings would come from the power to negotiate better prices with suppliers, given the bigger orders that will be placed.

However, he refused to rule out factory closures in the UK. Asked about the potential to move manufacturing out of Britain, he said the company may see “synergies play out” in manufacturing, distribution and media.

“When we think about getting more detail about those areas, it is probably detail yet to be shared,” he added.

News of the potential sale of iconic British brands Marmite and Colman’s sparked a backlash earlier this month. John Farrand, the managing director of the Guild of Fine Food, said the companies would “become a number on a spreadsheet.”

It follows other US takeovers of historic British brands such as Cadbury and Lea & Perrins.

The sale leaves Unilever focused on home and beauty brands such as Dove, Vaseline and Lynx.

Unilever recently demerged its ice cream business, spinning it out as The Magnum Ice Cream Company. The new entity, which was the result of an almost two-year-long process, is now home to Ben & Jerry’s and Cornetto, as well as the eponymous ice cream brand.

As well as a majority shareholding, Unilever will get $15.7bn in cash under the terms of the deal.

Mr Fernández said the McCormick deal was “another decisive step in sharpening our portfolio and accelerating our strategy towards high-growth categories”.

He said: “We are unlocking trapped value through a growth-led separation of foods, creating a scaled, global flavour powerhouse.

“By combining Unilever Foods’ iconic leading brands and global reach with McCormick’s exceptional portfolio, category expertise and capabilities, we are establishing a focused, high-quality business with significant top-line growth and value creation potential.”

However, shares in Unilever fell more than 7pc on news of the transaction.

Chris Beckett, an analyst at wealth manager Quilter Cheviot, said: “The market, so far, has not reacted well to the news.

“Completion is not expected until mid-2027, which is a long time and introduces risks that mean it could be stopped.”

Unilever has been on a major drive to trim down its sprawling portfolio of food brands.

It sold its spreads business in 2017, which included I Can’t Believe It’s Not Butter and Flora, and in 2022, it offloaded most of its tea business, including Lipton and PG Tips.

Brendan Foley, McCormick chairman, said: “The Unilever Foods business is one we have long admired, with a portfolio that complements our existing business, capabilities and long-term vision.

“This combination will create a diversified flavour leader with a robust growth profile that remains differentiated by its focus on flavouring calories while others compete for them.”

As well as French’s, a sweet mustard typically associated with hot dogs, McCormick also makes Frank’s Red Hot and Cholula hot sauces and various spice blends, including Old Bay seasoning.

data.title: Devon ‘war metal’ mine to help power Britain’s rearmament
data.description:
One of the largest tungsten deposits in the world is set to be mined in Devon as the Middle East crisis turbo-charges demand for the critical “war metal”.

The Hemerdon mine, near Plymouth, was discovered in 1867 and exploited through both world wars, as well as briefly from 2015 to 2018 before it was closed because of a lack of funds.

Now the site is being developed again by Tungsten West, with the wars in Ukraine and Iran triggering a surge in demand for munitions.

Tungsten is widely used in defence because of its tough, dense nature and its 3,422C melting point. The metal is used in everything from hypersonic missile nose cones to armour-piercing bullets, bunker-busting bombs and “airburst” counter-drone shells.

The price of the metal has risen sharply since China – which produces 80pc of global supplies – imposed export restrictions a year ago, prompting Western countries to seek alternate sources.

Prices also surged following the US attack on Iran in late February, as both America and Gulf countries deployed a large number of interceptors that use the metal.

Tungsten West is hoping to begin production of small amounts at Hemerdon later this year before ramping up to full capacity by 2027.

At peak production, the London-listed company estimates the site will produce around 3,300 tonnes of tungsten concentrate per year – or around 20pc of global supplies outside China.

That is enough to meet about a third of all defence-related demand globally.

Analysts have described the Devon deposits as among the largest untapped tungsten resources in the world.

Once fully up and running, the project – which will conduct mining operations for about 11 years – is expected to support more than 300 permanent jobs and generate hundreds of millions of pounds in economic value.

Nick Lawson, the boss of investment group Ocean Wall, said: “Hemerdon is one of the world’s great tungsten deposits and it sits in Devon.

“At a moment when Western supply chain vulnerability has become a strategic liability, Britain producing its own critical minerals is not an option, it is an obligation.”

Jeff Court, the chief executive of Tungsten West, said the mine represented a “very significant” chunk of the “non-China-sourced market”, adding that his customers “certainly understand the risk of being fully dependent, or highly dependent, on a China supply chain”.

“That is why they’re now looking at how to diversify,” he said.

While the mine will deliver a boost to defence, Mr Court said the mine’s primary buyers would be companies in industries such as construction, transport, manufacturing and medicine.

He added: “We’re a very good hedge and a good way to achieve supply chain resilience and support a variety of sectors, including defence, for a number of Western economies.

“As you can imagine, there’s a lot of people who were pretty keen for us to accelerate. So if we can accelerate and we can move faster, we’d like to bring forward production.”

The US has been attempting to rally allies behind initiatives to develop critical mineral supply chains that Beijing cannot strangle.

The Trump administration has also been striking deals around the world to invest in mines or processing facilities.

Mr Lawson said the search for alternatives to Chinese supplies had become “urgent” following Beijing’s decision to crack down on exports last year amid sparring over trade with Washington.

He said: “When China controls 83pc of global supply and chooses to weaponise that position, the rest of the world has no good options.”

Another tungsten project is also being developed in Cornwall by Strategic Minerals. 

The smaller Redmoor mine is undergoing detailed engineering surveys and has received £1.5m in government funding.

data.title: Labour bills taxpayers for £3bn public sector pension scheme
data.description:
Labour has handed taxpayers a £3bn pensions bill for a gold-plated scheme that could have paid for itself.

The Atomic Weapons Establishment (AWE) pension scheme, set up for workers who produce Britain’s nuclear warheads, holds enough assets to cover 92pc of its retirement promises.

However, ministers have decided to assume control of the funds and pay retirees from general taxation instead, which will cost the public around £2.9bn over the next three decades – or £284,000 a day.

The move, published deep in documents released after November’s Budget, will become law when the upcoming Pensions Schemes Bill receives Royal Assent later this year.

It echoes the 2012 Royal Mail fiasco, in which the Government took over the pension scheme’s assets, spent the proceeds and saddled taxpayers with a £45bn bill.

Neil Record, a former Bank of England economist, said the latest move was part of a “widespread, immoral pattern of government behaviour” that left future generations in “real financial trouble.”

AWE designs, manufactures and maintains the warheads carried by Britain’s nuclear submarines.

After being moved into private ownership in 1993, the company was brought back into Ministry of Defence ownership in 2021. Workers were moved out of their generous final salary pension scheme in 2017, but their payouts have a Crown guarantee that ensures they will be paid by the Government even if the scheme goes bust.

The scheme has taken £67.1m in bailouts since 2021, but its last valuation showed it could meet 92pc of its pension obligations.

According to calculations from Mr Record, retirees will receive around £2.9bn over the next 28 years, with the assets able to cover around £2.7bn. This includes projected investment returns of almost £1.5bn, which could rise even further if they perform well.

However, the scheme’s assets are now likely to be sold off, with the proceeds put towards general government spending – leaving taxpayers to fund pensioners’ payouts.

Mr Record said: “Under the watchful eye of the Office for Budget Responsibility (OBR), the Government has few ways in which it can increase spending without borrowing or taxing more. But finding and grabbing ‘forgotten assets’ like the AWE pension fund is a way to circumvent these constraints and spend more ‘pain-free’.

“It is a small part of a widespread, immoral pattern of government behaviour that seeks to push liabilities on to future taxpaying generations to prioritise the welfare of the current generation.

“The losers are the next generations of taxpayers, and along with the unsustainable national debt and unfunded pensions, these generations will be in real financial trouble.”

The move is a repeat of decisions made in 2012 by the Conservative government in the run-up to the sale of Royal Mail. In a bid to make the company attractive to bidders, ministers took over the pension scheme and provided a Crown guarantee for all retirement payouts accrued up to that date.

However, they also took control of the scheme’s £28bn of assets and spent the proceeds, leaving taxpayers to foot the bill for paying retirees. Last year, figures showed that Royal Mail pensions had already cost £16.5bn and there was still another £28.7bn left to pay.

Ian Mills, of consultants Barnett Waddingham, said: “What is happening with AWE is similar to what happened with Royal Mail. By taking the assets out of the pension scheme, the Government is able to liquidate them in any way it sees fit. That marginally reduces its need to raise finance elsewhere, such as through borrowing.

“However, the economics is pretty much the same as before, as once those assets have been sold the pensions are still going to have to be paid and the Government, or the taxpayer, is on the hook for them.

“It’s basically just moving money from left pocket to right pocket and makes the numbers look a bit better in the short term.”

A government spokesman said that taxpayers would face no new liabilities over the AWE decision, but did not comment on the loss of £1.5bn in potential investment returns.

He said: “This will not create any new risk or liability for taxpayers or to members of the scheme, and will strengthen the long-term security of members’ benefits.

“AWE’s employees play a crucial role in supporting the UK’s national security at the forefront of innovation, supporting our nuclear deterrent by designing, developing, manufacturing, and maintaining our sovereign nuclear warheads.”

data.title: Net tightens around Nvidia over Chinese chip smugglers
data.description:
Days before Christmas, a US government customs officer conducted what he believed would be a routine warehouse inspection.

Inside the vast facility were thousands of boxes, all stacked full of computer servers. Everything seemed in order.

When the inspector finally left, though, those in the warehouse breathed a sigh of relief.

Just hours earlier, they had been racing to fix fake labels to the boxes, carefully melting off old serial numbers using a red hairdryer, before sticking on new ones.

Inside the crates were thousands of worthless pieces of dummy server equipment. The original contents – $500m (£379m) of powerful Nvidia AI chips – had already been shipped illegally to China, according to US government charges, in a breach of US export bans.

In total, the scheme was worth $2.5bn, US prosecutors claimed. As many as 100 people had been hired to stage the elaborate warehouse con, which prosecutors alleged was organised by Wally Liaw, one of the co-founders of US-listed technology business Supermicro. Liaw pleaded not guilty last week to the charges made against him.

Supermicro said it had placed two staff members on leave and cut ties with a contractor over the investigation. The company is not a defendant in the case and has not been accused of wrongdoing.

It said the three people charged sought to deceive its internal compliance officers, adding that the “conduct by these individuals alleged in the indictment is a contravention of the company’s policies and compliance controls, including efforts to circumvent applicable export control laws and regulations”.

A spokesman for the company said: “Supermicro maintains a robust compliance programme and is committed to full adherence to all applicable US export and re-export control laws and regulations.”

However, when news of the charges broke, Supermicro’s shares dropped by a third.

Now, US politicians are turning their attention to Nvidia, whose cutting-edge graphics processing units – or GPUs – have proved to be the ultimate prize for technology smugglers seeking to cash in on China’s insatiable demand for AI chips.

In a series of cases, smugglers have targeted Nvidia’s technology, with prosecutors claiming middlemen in the US and shell companies in Asia are being used to bypass customs controls and ultimately pass on AI chips to China.

Earlier this month, Senator Elizabeth Warren, a Democrat, and Senator Jim Banks, a Republican, wrote to Howard Lutnick, the US commerce secretary, demanding a “full review” of Nvidia’s licences to customers in Asia.

The letter alleged that Nvidia’s compliance programme was “grossly inadequate to protect cutting-edge American technology from foreign-adversary access”.

It also claimed that Jensen Huang, Nvidia’s chief executive, may have sought to mislead US officials by saying last May that there was “no evidence of any AI chip diversion”.

Huang had argued that the weight of Nvidia’s latest AI systems meant smugglers would not be hiding it “in your pocket or your backpack anytime soon”.

At the same time, Huang has lobbied hard for the US to restart sales of its chips to China.

In April last year, Nvidia put out a statement accusing critics of “tall tales” and scaremongering about chip smuggling.

These included a claim by Anthropic, the developer of the Claude AI bot, that smugglers were trying everything from hiding chips in prosthetic baby bumps to stacking them alongside live lobster shipments to avoid detection.

The statement has aged poorly.

As well as the Supermicro scheme, US customs agents last year seized $50m in cash and Nvidia hardware from one business as part of a scheme to smuggle chips to China. The company Hao Global and its owner Alan Hao Hsu plead guilty to smuggling and unlawful export activities.

The US Justice Department claimed he was part of a “China-linked AI tech smuggling network” which exported or attempted to export $160m in chips.

In another case, unsealed this month, three alleged conspirators attempted to trick Nvidia’s compliance teams into approving the sale of $170m in chips to Thailand, which were secretly intended for China. In a group chat between the alleged smugglers, one warned: “DO NOT MENTION ANYTHING ABOUT CHINA.”

In that instance, Nvidia blocked the attempted purchase.

A source familiar with the case said Nvidia had seen “red flags” in the shipments and refused to sign off on them. Nvidia made efforts to verify the final customer on the deal, halting the purchase when its queries raised more questions than answers.

Jack Burnham, an analyst at the Foundation for Defense of Democracies, a non-profit, claimed that the string of cases “highlight a massive failure of industry self-policing that places US national security at risk”.

“Smuggling is part and parcel of China’s efforts to obtain American hardware. Congress needs to step in when industry won’t.”

While some smugglers have been stopped in their tracks, the Supermicro case alone would account for about 1pc of Nvidia’s total 2025 sales, which were worth $215bn. Sales in its latest quarter were about $68.1bn.

Richard Windsor, an independent technology analyst, says the sums are “mostly a drop in the ocean” compared to its overall business.

He adds that the relatively small number of chips making it around the export bans are useful for Chinese AI labs, but they would “not help in any way when it comes to rolling out AI services at scale”.

Donald Trump, the US president, has sought to ease chip restrictions on China to allow sales of Nvidia’s less powerful technology to recommence. However, China hawks in the Senate have sought to keep up the pressure and cut out any potential routes to the country over fears Beijing’s AI ambitions threaten America’s security.

Last week, a US House of Representatives committee advanced the Chips Security Act. This proposed law would require Nvidia to introduce tracking technology on its chips to prevent them from being trafficked.

Nvidia has pushed back on the suggestion it would ever impose some kind of “kill switch” or “spyware” on its chips.

Meanwhile, Senator Tom Cotton, a Republican and hardline China hawk, this month wrote to Lutnick raising questions about another major Nvidia customer, Singapore’s Megaspeed. This company emerged from obscurity to become one of the biggest buyers of Nvidia chips with billions of dollars worth of orders.

Megaspeed is now the subject of a police investigation in Singapore, according to officials in the country. It was contacted for comment.

It is understood that Nvidia has conducted multiple visits to Megaspeed to ensure that its chips are being used as intended.

US officials may go further to clamp down on foreign use of Nvidia’s technology. Some senators have called for restrictions on remote access to graphics chips in overseas data centres.

While supplies of physical chips are being curtailed, there is little to stop a data centre company in Asia simply renting digital access to its chips to a Chinese customer.

“The biggest workaround by China is the import of compute,” says Windsor. “They lease a huge data centre in Thailand or Vietnam and run the compute there.”

Nvidia has sought to quash concerns. A spokesman said its customers “are under express instructions not to provide any controlled servers, support or service without approval from the US government”, adding: “Our strict diligence process led to prosecutions of would-be smugglers, and we continue to work with the government to enforce the rules.”

The spokesman also said: “Strict compliance is a top priority for Nvidia. We continue to work closely with our customers and the government on compliance programs as export regulations have expanded.

“Unlawful diversion of controlled US computers to China is a losing proposition across the board – Nvidia does not provide any service or support for such systems, and the enforcement mechanisms are rigorous and effective.”

Still, having previously dismissed graphics chip smuggling as a “tall tale”, cases such as the $2.5bn con last Christmas are forcing a change in approach.

Today, Huang and Nvidia are eager to show they are serious about shutting down an apparently booming black market. US officials will be watching closely.

data.title: The green-belt village about to fall under the shadow of Europe’s largest data centre
data.description:
“The peace, quiet and openness of the country.” That’s why Danny Leach and his family first moved to North Ockendon, on the north-east side of Greater London, back in 2005.

Despite being barely outside the M25 and a part of the London Borough of Havering, the village and surrounding countryside are remarkably quiet.

The fenland surrounding the village is undeveloped and, considering its proximity to the capital, surprisingly biodiverse.

“I’ve got nearly 100 species of birds that visit my garden, including kingfishers, barn owls and tawny owls,” says Leach. “There are also great crested newts and foxes. On a summer evening, I can hear the frogs croaking.”

Part of the reason such an environment can exist so close to a city of 10 million is the Metropolitan Green Belt, which, since its creation in 1938, has protected the countryside near London and prevented urban sprawl.

Now, a proposed Green Belt-busting development could change the area’s character forever.

Havering council recently launched a consultation on “East Havering Data Centre”, a hyperscale AI facility that would make North Ockendon home to the largest data centre in Europe.

The facility would use 540 acres of existing farmland and require 600 megawatts of electricity to operate – the same amount of energy needed to run one million homes.

Developer Digital Reef says the site will include around 84 acres of data centre buildings, along with a 280-acre ecological park.

Residents were outraged to learn that the consultation period for the 2,700-page proposal would last only 30 days, clashing with the May elections, and that Havering council were processing the project via a “Local Development Order”, a special type of planning tool that allows conventional planning permission steps to be skipped.

Labour and Conservative members of the council’s strategic planning committee advocated a longer, 12-week consultation period, but were outvoted by the Havering Residents Association, a local party that runs the council.

Data centres are essentially large warehouses filled with specialist computing equipment that processes AI requests submitted by users on their phones or computers. East Havering Data Centre is one of hundreds of these projects proposed near Britain’s major cities to power the growing demand for AI, many of which will be built on green-belt land.

Data centres require large amounts of energy and water to operate, raising concerns that construction plans could put pressure on electricity and water infrastructure and drive up household bills.

Leach is one of many North Ockendon residents who are unhappy with the proposal. His property, which backs on to the site, will be particularly affected.

‘People move to the country for a reason’

“It’s a big shock,” he says. “People move to the countryside for a reason, and then suddenly you have something the size of a battleship going up at the end of your garden.”

Local businesses are also worried. Daniel Anthony has run Top Meadow Golf Club his entire working life, taking over from his father.

Since launching a nine-hole golf club in 1985, the family has grown the business into an 18-hole club and 20-room hotel that employs 50 people.

“Everything, everything, everything has been reinvested from day one,” says Anthony. He is worried the project is putting a lifetime of work at risk.

Today, Anthony says the club makes most of its money from functions hosted at the hotel and restaurant: “We do anything from birthdays and anniversaries to funerals, which are a big one for us because it’s only two and a half miles to the nearest crematorium.”

East Havering Data Centre will be built directly across the road from Top Meadow. Anthony is worried that the facility will be a blight on the club’s prized views of the countryside. “It’s the view really; that’s what people come for,” he says.

He is also concerned about industrial traffic and possible traffic diversions during the construction. A traffic diversion could extend the journey from Top Meadow to the nearby crematorium from two and a half to 10 miles, threatening the club’s funeral and wake business.

The rise of data centres

Developer Digital Reef estimates the facility will take four years to construct – longer than most data centre projects.

Ed Griffiths, of construction consultancy Barbour ABI, says construction is relatively fast for most data centres, with build programmes averaging 19 to 30 months, depending on scale and power requirements.

Sites near large cities are ideal for AI data centres, as user requests can be processed more quickly.

“Urban fringe locations are common for data centres, particularly where they provide proximity to users while still offering enough space and infrastructure to support large facilities,” says Griffiths.

This makes the green-belt land that surrounds many of Britain’s cities tempting for developers.

Data provided to The Telegraph by Barbour ABI indicate that 13 of the 100 largest data centres planned in the UK would fall within the green belt.

These green-belt data centres have a combined value of just over £10bn, representing an outsized share of the 100 largest planned projects at 19pc of the £52bn total.

Data centre construction is a priority for the Government, which views the facilities as essential for turning Britain into an “AI superpower” and for growing the economy.

In a January 2025 speech, Sir Keir Starmer said AI will be “mainlined into the veins of the nation” and has classed data centres as “critical national infrastructure”.

‘Destroying a rich ecosystem’

Many developers include environmental concessions, such as the East Havering Data Centre’s “ecology park” in London, in their planning applications as a sweetener for nearby communities.

However, Ian Pirie, a Havering Friends of the Earth co-ordinator, is sceptical of their benefits.

“Developers think that they can just tear up all that land and then dig somewhere else, plant some trees, and they’ve restored the ecosystem. It’s complete nonsense,” says Pirie.

“They are destroying an already existing, rich ecosystem. It would take another 100 years to have what we’ve got there now.”

Another local concern is the data centre’s potential water usage. The facilities often use water cooling to prevent computers from overheating.

Digital Reef has proposed that excess heat generated by the data centre will be redirected to heat local homes and a novel 12-acre indoor avocado farm.

But residents are unconvinced, telling The Telegraph that the local water supply is already temperamental and that industrial usage could push it to breaking point.

A spokesman for North Ockendon Residents’ Association says: “The water supply here is very old, under-maintained pipe work. If they were to then start taking thousands of gallons a day and something happened, we would be without water.”

Griffiths, who works at Barbour ABI, says access to grid power is the most important factor in selecting a site for a data centre, followed by water availability.

Data centres’ use of energy and water resources could have a national impact. Ireland, which is a hub for trans-Atlantic data cables and hosts the European headquarters of American tech giants such as Amazon and Google, has prioritised its data centre infrastructure.

In 2024, Ireland’s data centres used 22pc of its total electricity supply, more than the 18pc used by the country’s urban homes, according to the Central Statistics Office. There are concerns that a similar build-out in the UK would drive up energy costs, squeezing consumers.

A spokesman for Havering Local Planning Authority says that a full evidence base for East Havering Data Centre would be considered as part of the application, including assessments on biodiversity, ecology, economic impact, transport, water usage and use of the green belt. 

Digital Reef could not be reached for comment.
